name: Battle of Fort Necessity
description: On July 3, 1754, a young George Washington and his Virginia militia were overwhelmed by French and Indian forces at this hastily built circular stockade in the Pennsylvania wilderness, marking the opening land engagement of the French and Indian War. Washington was forced to surrender — the only time in his career — after a day-long battle fought in pouring rain that left a third of his men killed or wounded, with the French allowing his force to withdraw. Today the site is preserved as Fort Necessity National Battlefield near Farmington, PA, with a reconstructed stockade and an interpretive visitor center managed by the National Park Service. While the park itself prohibits metal detecting, the surrounding Great Meadows area saw extensive 18th-century military encampment activity, and private land in the broader region has yielded musket balls, brass buttons, and military hardware.

name: Battle of the Monongahela (Braddock's Defeat)
description: On July 9, 1755, British General Edward Braddock led a column of 1,400 regulars and colonial troops through a wooded ravine near present-day Pittsburgh, where roughly 900 French soldiers and Native American warriors ambushed them in one of the most catastrophic defeats in British military history. Braddock himself was mortally wounded, and the British column — unable to adapt to frontier warfare — suffered over 900 casualties before Washington rallied the survivors in retreat. The battlefield near Braddock, PA sits largely beneath modern development, though a small monument marks the general area where Braddock fell. Scattered finds of Brown Bess musket balls, British regimental buttons, and military accoutrements have surfaced along the route on private land, and the broader Monongahela River valley saw extensive military movement throughout the war.

name: Battle of Lake George
description: On September 8, 1755, a combined force of British colonials and Mohawk warriors under Sir William Johnson defeated a French and Canadian army commanded by Baron Dieskau near the southern end of Lake George in New York, halting the French advance toward Albany and the Hudson Valley. Dieskau was captured during the engagement, which saw savage fighting including a French ambush of a colonial detachment that became known as 'Bloody Morning Scout.' The battlefield area is now largely absorbed into the Lake George Battlefield Park and surrounding resort town, with the lake itself — once a major military corridor — hosting a popular tourist industry. Musket balls, French trade silver, and military buttons have been found in the lake region over the years, and private lands along the lakeshore saw heavy encampment use throughout the French and Indian War.

name: Battle of Ticonderoga 1758
description: In July 1758, British General James Abercrombie led the largest army ever assembled in North America to that point — nearly 15,000 men — against the undermanned French garrison at Fort Carillon (later Fort Ticonderoga), only to be repulsed with devastating losses in one of Britain's worst defeats of the war. French commander the Marquis de Montcalm had erected a hasty breastwork of felled timber that shredded the massed British and Highland infantry assaults, killing or wounding nearly 2,000 men in a matter of hours. Fort Ticonderoga stands today as a privately operated historic site with a reconstructed fortress and museum on the Lake Champlain peninsula in New York, open to visitors. Metal detecting is prohibited on the fort grounds, but the surrounding slopes and approach routes have produced French musket balls, British regimental buttons, and military equipment on private property.

name: Battle of Fort William Henry
description: In August 1757, French commander Montcalm besieged and captured the British Fort William Henry at the southern tip of Lake George, New York, after a six-day siege that forced the garrison's surrender under agreed terms of parole. What followed was one of the war's most infamous episodes: Montcalm's Native American allies, unpaid in plunder, attacked the surrendering British column and massacred between 100 and 200 soldiers and civilians, with hundreds more taken captive. The site is now occupied by a reconstructed fort and museum at Lake George, NY, operated as a living history attraction. Metal detecting is restricted on the historic site, but the lake bottom and surrounding shoreline have produced 18th-century military artifacts including musket balls, buttons, and personal effects from the extensive French and Indian War campaigns along this waterway.

name: Battle of Fort Frontenac
description: In August 1758, a British colonial force of roughly 3,000 men under Colonel John Bradstreet made a daring amphibious raid across Lake Ontario to capture Fort Frontenac at present-day Kingston, Ontario — the vital French supply and communications hub connecting Montreal to the western forts. The small French garrison surrendered quickly, and Bradstreet's men destroyed nine armed vessels, enormous stores of supplies, and the fort itself, effectively crippling France's ability to supply her western allies and garrison. The site is now within the downtown core of Kingston, Ontario, with a small park and interpretive marker noting the fort's former location. Archaeological excavations have recovered 18th-century military hardware, French cannon balls, trade goods, and military buttons from the site over the years.

name: Battle of Beauport
description: On July 31, 1759, British General James Wolfe launched a costly amphibious assault against the fortified French shoreline at Beauport, near Quebec City, attempting to find a way past Montcalm's defensive lines before the campaigning season ended. The attack was repulsed with significant British losses as French and Canadian defenders poured fire down on the landing troops from elevated positions, demonstrating the near-impregnability of the Beauport shore. This defeat pushed Wolfe to find an alternative approach, ultimately leading to the daring night ascent at Anse-au-Foulon and the decisive battle on the Plains of Abraham six weeks later. The Beauport shoreline is now largely developed residential and industrial land east of Quebec City, though the area saw intense military activity during the 1759 siege campaign.

name: Battle of the Plains of Abraham
description: On September 13, 1759, British General James Wolfe led approximately 4,500 soldiers up the cliffs at Anse-au-Foulon before dawn and deployed them on the Plains of Abraham outside the walls of Quebec City, forcing the Marquis de Montcalm to abandon his defensive positions and give battle on open ground. In a brief but decisive engagement lasting perhaps 20 minutes, disciplined British volleys broke the advancing French lines; both Wolfe and Montcalm were mortally wounded, and Quebec surrendered five days later, effectively ending French rule in Canada. The battlefield is now Battlefields Park (Parc des Champs-de-Bataille) in Quebec City, a large urban park maintained by the National Battlefields Commission, with interpretive panels marking key positions. Detecting is prohibited in the park, but the cliffs, landing areas, and surrounding countryside saw massive military movements throughout the 1759 siege campaign.

name: Battle of Fort Niagara
description: In July 1759, a British force under Sir William Johnson besieged the strategically vital Fort Niagara at the mouth of the Niagara River on Lake Ontario, cutting off the French supply line to the entire Great Lakes interior. A French relief force marching from the south was ambushed and destroyed at the Battle of La Belle Famille on July 24, and the fort surrendered the following day — a loss that effectively ended French power in the Great Lakes region. Old Fort Niagara is today a beautifully preserved state historic site in Youngstown, NY, with original 18th-century stone buildings including the famous 'French Castle' still standing. Metal detecting is prohibited on the fort grounds, but the surrounding Niagara frontier and lakeshore saw extensive military traffic during the French and Indian War, with private land in the area occasionally yielding 18th-century military artifacts.

name: Battle of Bushy Run
description: On August 5-6, 1763, British Colonel Henry Bouquet and his column of Highlanders and Royal Americans were ambushed by Delaware, Shawnee, and Mingo warriors near Bushy Run Station in western Pennsylvania while marching to relieve the besieged Fort Pitt. Bouquet feigned retreat on the second day to lure the warriors into an ambush of his own, decisively defeating them in a battle that helped bring Pontiac's War to a close in the east. The battlefield is preserved as Bushy Run Battlefield, a Pennsylvania state historic site in Harrison City, with trails, earthworks, and a museum. Metal detecting is not permitted on state historic sites, but the surrounding countryside and the route of Bouquet's march through the Pennsylvania backcountry offer private land opportunities where musket balls, brass buttons, and military hardware have been found.

name: Battle of Bloody Run
description: On July 31, 1763, Captain James Dalzell led 247 British soldiers on a night sortie from Detroit to strike the encampment of Pontiac's besieging warriors, but walked into a devastating ambush at Parent's Creek near the city. The warriors, well aware of the British plan, poured fire from both banks of the creek, killing 20 soldiers and wounding another 34 in a rout that sent the survivors fleeing back to the fort — the creek reportedly ran red with blood, giving the engagement its grim name. The battle site is now entirely within the urban fabric of modern Detroit, with Parent's Creek culverted and the area long since developed. No detecting opportunities exist at the urban site, but the broader Detroit River corridor and the region's private farmland saw extensive 18th-century military and trade activity.

name: Battle of Fort Pitt
description: During Pontiac's War in 1763, Delaware and Shawnee warriors besieged the British-held Fort Pitt at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers — a strategic position that would become the city of Pittsburgh. The garrison, commanded by Captain Simeon Ecuyer, held out for two months through escalating pressure until Bouquet's relief column broke the siege after the Battle of Bushy Run. Fort Pitt's location is now Point State Park in downtown Pittsburgh, with a reconstructed bastion and the Fort Pitt Museum operated by the Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. The park prohibits metal detecting, but the rivers and surrounding hillsides of the Pittsburgh metropolitan area saw three centuries of military and commercial activity, and private land searches in the outlying areas have yielded 18th-century trade goods and military hardware.

name: Battle of Lexington and Concord
description: On April 19, 1775, the 'shot heard round the world' was fired on Lexington Green when British Regulars under Lieutenant Colonel Francis Smith confronted a company of Minute Men, killing eight colonists in a brief skirmish before marching on to Concord. At Concord's North Bridge, the Minute Men opened fire — the first organized armed resistance of the American Revolution — and the British column suffered a punishing running fight along the sixteen miles back to Boston, losing 273 men killed and wounded. The sites are preserved as Minute Man National Historical Park in Lexington, Concord, and Lincoln, MA, one of the best-interpreted battlefields in America with original stone walls, the North Bridge reconstruction, and the Battle Road trail. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but private properties along the original Bay Road corridor have yielded Continental-era musket balls, pewter buttons, and British military hardware.

name: Battle of Bunker Hill
description: On June 17, 1775, colonial militia defending hastily constructed earthworks on Breed's Hill (the main fighting position, often confused with adjacent Bunker Hill) twice repulsed massed British assaults before falling back on the third charge after running out of powder, inflicting over 1,000 British casualties — nearly 40% of the attacking force — in the costliest engagement of the war for the British. The battle proved that American militia could stand against professional Redcoats and stiffened colonial resolve, even though the British technically captured the hill. Bunker Hill Monument, a 221-foot granite obelisk in Charlestown, MA, dominates the skyline above the neighborhood that grew over the battlefield. The entire area is now urban Boston, with detecting prohibited, but the original earthwork outlines are partially preserved and the site is managed by Boston National Historical Park.

name: Battle of Fort Ticonderoga 1775
description: In the predawn hours of May 10, 1775, Ethan Allen and his Green Mountain Boys — joined by Benedict Arnold — surprised and captured the sleeping British garrison at Fort Ticonderoga on Lake Champlain in New York, seizing the fort without a shot fired. The fort's 59 artillery pieces were later dragged to Boston by Henry Knox, where they forced the British evacuation of the city in March 1776. Fort Ticonderoga is today a privately operated historic site with original 18th-century masonry, a world-class museum collection, and ongoing archaeological programs on the Lake Champlain peninsula. Metal detecting is not permitted at the fort site, but the surrounding lake shores and approach roads saw heavy military traffic from both the French and Indian War and the Revolution, and private lands in the area have yielded musket balls, British and American buttons, and accoutrements.

name: Battle of Breed's Hill
description: The main defensive position during the Battle of Bunker Hill on June 17, 1775, Breed's Hill saw colonial militia dig earthworks overnight and then hold them against two massive British frontal assaults before ammunition ran out and the position was lost on the third charge. Colonel William Prescott reportedly ordered his men 'Don't fire until you see the whites of their eyes,' and the devastating volleys killed or wounded a significant portion of the British assault force, including many officers. The Bunker Hill Monument stands atop the site in Charlestown, MA, as part of Boston National Historical Park; the battlefield is entirely within a dense urban neighborhood. No metal detecting is permitted, but construction projects in the Charlestown area over the years have uncovered period artifacts including musket balls and military hardware.

name: Siege of Boston
description: From April 1775 to March 1776, the Continental Army besieged the British garrison in Boston after Lexington and Concord, in the first major sustained military operation of the Revolutionary War. The siege was resolved when Henry Knox arrived with artillery captured at Fort Ticonderoga and emplaced them on Dorchester Heights, forcing the British to evacuate by sea on March 17, 1776 — an event still celebrated as Evacuation Day in Massachusetts. The siege lines, redoubts, and encampments once spread across Roxbury, Cambridge, Charlestown, and surrounding towns, virtually all of which are now within the modern Boston metropolitan area. Various earthwork remnants survive in parks around the city, and private land in the outer siege lines area has occasionally yielded Continental Army buttons, musket balls, and camp debris.

name: Battle of Quebec 1775
description: On the night of December 31, 1775, American General Richard Montgomery and Colonel Benedict Arnold launched a desperate two-pronged assault on the fortified city of Quebec in a blizzard, attempting to complete the conquest of Canada before their troops' enlistments expired on New Year's Day. Montgomery was killed almost immediately, Arnold was badly wounded, and Daniel Morgan led the other column deep into the city's Lower Town before being surrounded and captured — the Americans suffered over 400 casualties and prisoners while the British garrison held the city. The battle site within Quebec City's historic Lower Town and the walls are now part of the UNESCO World Heritage Old Quebec area, one of the best-preserved historic fortifications in North America. The walled city and nearby Plains of Abraham are managed by Parks Canada, with detecting prohibited, though the surrounding countryside saw extensive 18th-century military traffic.

name: Battle of Moore's Creek Bridge
description: On February 27, 1776, North Carolina Patriot militia ambushed a column of 1,600 Loyalist Highland Scots and regulators at Moore's Creek Bridge near Wilmington, NC, killing dozens and capturing over 800 men including all their officers in a brief but decisive engagement. The Loyalists, armed largely with broadswords and their Highland weapons, crossed a partially dismantled bridge into withering fire from Patriots concealed on the opposite bank, and the rout effectively ended Loyalist organized resistance in the Carolinas for years. The site is preserved as Moores Creek National Battlefield near Currie, NC, with reconstructed earthworks, interpretive trails, and a visitor center operated by the National Park Service. Metal detecting is prohibited within the park, but the surrounding coastal plain farmland saw extensive Patriot and Loyalist movement throughout the war, and private land searches have recovered Revolutionary War-era buttons and musket balls.

name: Battle of Long Island
description: On August 27, 1776, British General William Howe executed a brilliantly planned envelopment of the American lines on Brooklyn Heights, sending 10,000 men through the unguarded Jamaica Pass while Hessians and British regulars pinned the American front, resulting in approximately 2,000 American casualties and prisoners in the largest battle of the Revolutionary War. Washington skillfully evacuated his 9,000-man army across the East River to Manhattan under cover of night and fog, saving the army that would fight on to ultimate victory. The battlefield is now almost entirely within the densely developed borough of Brooklyn, NY, with small remnants at Prospect Park and Old Stone House Historic Interpretive Center marking where fierce rear-guard fighting took place. No detecting opportunities exist in the urban environment, but the Old Stone House and Battle Pass area provide historical interpretation of this near-fatal defeat.

name: Battle of Harlem Heights
description: On September 16, 1776, the day after the British landing on Manhattan, a skirmish on the high ground of Harlem Heights gave the Continental Army its first small victory of the New York campaign, as American riflemen and light infantry pushed back British and Hessian troops in a sharp engagement that boosted badly needed morale. Washington's army had been driven off the Brooklyn Heights and then from lower Manhattan, but the fighting on the heights — roughly around today's 125th Street — demonstrated that his soldiers could fight effectively in the right terrain. The battlefield is entirely within the densely built upper Manhattan neighborhood of Morningside Heights and Harlem, with no visible remains. The broader Manhattan campaign saw British and Hessian forces scatter countless musket balls, buttons, and camp debris across the island, little of which survives given the extensive development.

name: Battle of White Plains
description: On October 28, 1776, British and Hessian forces under General Howe attacked Washington's entrenched position at White Plains, NY, successfully flanking the American position on Chatterton's Hill after hard fighting but allowing Washington to withdraw his army northward rather than destroy it. The battle demonstrated both the tactical skill of Howe's army and Washington's ability to avoid the decisive engagement the British sought — a pattern that would define much of the New York and New Jersey campaign. The battlefield area is within the modern city of White Plains and the Greenburgh area of Westchester County, NY, mostly developed but with the White Plains Battlefield State Historic Site preserving a small parcel. Detecting is restricted on state historic sites, but private land surrounding the battle area occasionally yields Hessian and Continental-era buttons, musket balls, and camp artifacts.

name: Battle of Fort Washington
description: On November 16, 1776, British and Hessian forces under General Howe captured Fort Washington in upper Manhattan in a coordinated assault from three directions, taking nearly 2,800 American prisoners — the largest single capture of Continental troops in the entire war. The fall of the fort, followed three days later by the fall of Fort Lee across the Hudson, effectively ended American control of New York and forced Washington's desperate retreat across New Jersey. The site of Fort Washington is now Bennett Park in the Washington Heights neighborhood of upper Manhattan, the highest natural point on the island, where an outline of the original earthworks is embedded in the park surface. No detecting is permitted in the city park, but the surrounding neighborhood and the broader Washington Heights area saw intensive military operations throughout the fall of 1776.

name: Battle of Trenton
description: In the early morning hours of December 26, 1776, General Washington led 2,400 Continentals across the ice-choked Delaware River in a blizzard to launch a surprise assault on the Hessian garrison at Trenton, NJ, killing or capturing nearly 900 professional German soldiers in a stunning reversal of American fortunes. The victory — following months of devastating defeats — revitalized the cause of American independence and convinced many soldiers to reenlist. The crossing and battle sites are commemorated at Washington Crossing Historic Park (straddling the Pennsylvania-New Jersey border) and Trenton Battle Monument in New Jersey, managed by both states. Metal detecting is prohibited at the historic parks, but the Delaware River ferry crossings, approach roads, and surrounding farmland along the New Jersey banks saw intensive military traffic in late 1776 and early 1777.

name: Battle of Assunpink Creek
description: On January 2, 1777, Washington's army repulsed three British probing attacks across Assunpink Creek in Trenton, NJ, in the 'Second Battle of Trenton,' holding the south bank against Cornwallis's pursuing army after the first Trenton victory. That night, Washington executed a brilliant strategic maneuver — leaving campfires burning and quietly withdrawing his army around the British left flank — to march on Princeton the next morning, keeping the New Jersey campaign alive. The battlefield is in central Trenton, NJ, now an urban and park area around the confluence of the Assunpink Creek and the Delaware River. The broad area of Washington's remarkable ten-day campaign through Trenton and Princeton has produced Revolutionary War-era finds on private land, though the creek crossing and park areas are protected.

name: Battle of Princeton
description: On January 3, 1777, Washington's force — having slipped around Cornwallis's army overnight from Trenton — struck the British rear guard at Princeton, NJ, in a sharp dawn battle that saw General Hugh Mercer mortally wounded before Washington himself rode forward to rally his troops and rout the British column. The victory, combined with the two Trenton fights, effectively cleared New Jersey of British winter occupation and marked a turning point in the morale of the Continental cause. Princeton Battlefield State Park preserves the Clarke House (used as a field hospital), the graveyard, and the open farmland where the battle was fought, with interpretive markers. Metal detecting is prohibited in the state park, but the broader Princeton and Mercer County area has yielded period musket balls, British and Hessian buttons, and accoutrements on private agricultural land.

name: Battle of Fort Ticonderoga 1777
description: In early July 1777, British General John Burgoyne's army during its invasion from Canada placed artillery on Sugar Loaf Hill — a commanding height overlooking the fort that the Americans had considered inaccessible — forcing the American garrison under General Arthur St. Clair to abandon Fort Ticonderoga in a hasty overnight retreat without a fight. The loss of the 'Gibraltar of the North' shocked Americans and enraged Congress, but the retreat preserved St. Clair's army to fight in subsequent actions that ultimately contributed to Burgoyne's defeat at Saratoga. Fort Ticonderoga is a privately operated historic site in Ticonderoga, NY, with original 18th-century masonry, an extensive museum, and ongoing archaeology. Detecting is not allowed at the fort site, though the surrounding Lake Champlain corridor has seen extensive private land finds of French and Indian War and Revolutionary-era military hardware.

name: Battle of Hubbardton
description: On July 7, 1777, the American rear guard under Colonels Seth Warner and Ebenezer Francis turned to fight off the pursuing British advance under General Fraser near Hubbardton, VT, in the only Revolutionary War battle fought on Vermont soil. Though the Americans were ultimately forced to retreat after a fierce engagement, they inflicted significant casualties on the British and delayed Burgoyne's advance, buying time for St. Clair's retreating force to escape. The Hubbardton Battlefield State Historic Site is a remarkably well-preserved property in East Hubbardton, VT, with original terrain, interpretive markers, and a small visitor center operated by the Vermont Division for Historic Preservation. Detecting is not permitted on the state historic site, but the surrounding Green Mountain woodland and farm country, far from major development, preserves the landscape much as it appeared in 1777.

name: Battle of Oriskany
description: On August 6, 1777, a Patriot militia force of 800 men under General Nicholas Herkimer marching to relieve the siege of Fort Stanwix was ambushed by 400 Loyalists and Mohawk warriors under Joseph Brant and Sir John Johnson in a narrow ravine near Oriskany, NY, resulting in one of the bloodiest battles of the Revolution. Herkimer was mortally wounded but continued to direct the battle from under a tree, and after a two-hour close-quarters struggle the ambushed column fought the attackers to a standstill — stopping the British advance in the Mohawk Valley. The Oriskany Battlefield State Historic Site in Oriskany, NY, preserves a portion of the ravine and the monument to those who fell, with interpretive trails. Metal detecting is prohibited at the state historic site, but the surrounding Mohawk Valley farmland, scene of extensive warfare from 1777 through 1781, has produced Brown Bess musket balls, Continental and British buttons, and personal items on private land.

name: Battle of Bennington
description: On August 16, 1777, General John Stark's New Hampshire militia and Vermont Green Mountain Boys attacked a foraging column of Hessians, Loyalists, and British regulars under Lieutenant Colonel Friedrich Baum near Bennington, VT (actually fought in present-day New York state), routing the column and then destroying the reinforcements arriving under Breymann in a second engagement. The twin victories killed or captured over 900 of Burgoyne's troops and contributed critically to the isolation and eventual surrender of his army at Saratoga two months later. The Bennington Battlefield State Historic Site in Walloomsac, NY, preserves a portion of the terrain with interpretive markers and a view from Baum's Hill. Metal detecting is prohibited at the state historic site, but the surrounding Walloomsac River valley farmland — where Hessian and militia units clashed — has produced period musket balls, Hessian brass buttons, and military equipment on private property.

name: Battle of Brandywine
description: On September 11, 1777, General Howe again outflanked Washington's army at Brandywine Creek in Pennsylvania, sending the bulk of his force on a wide sweep through an unguarded ford while pinning the Americans frontally, routing the Continental right wing and nearly capturing the army. Despite the defeat — the largest single-day engagement of the Revolution — Washington preserved his force and Lafayette, wounded in the leg, distinguished himself rallying the troops. Brandywine Battlefield Park in Chadds Ford, PA, preserves the Chad House (Lafayette's headquarters) and the Birmingham Hill fighting area, with ongoing archaeological programs identifying the extent of the battlefield. Metal detecting is prohibited in the state historic park, but private farmland in the broad Brandywine Valley corridor has yielded British and Hessian buttons, musket balls, pewter items, and camp debris from the extensive operations in 1777.

name: Battle of Freeman's Farm (First Saratoga)
description: On September 19, 1777, the first of two battles at Saratoga saw American riflemen and Continentals under Daniel Morgan and Henry Dearborn contest Burgoyne's advancing columns in the cleared farmland of Freeman's Farm near the Hudson River, fighting the British to a costly standstill in a grueling all-day engagement. Burgoyne held the field but his army had suffered over 600 casualties it could not replace, and the fight demonstrated the growing effectiveness of the Continental Army. Saratoga National Historical Park in Stillwater, NY, preserves 3,000 acres of the battlefield with driving tours, period earthworks, and the Boot Monument marking where Arnold's leg was wounded. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding Saratoga County farmland has produced Continental and British buttons, musket balls, and artillery fragments on private land.

name: Battle of Germantown
description: On October 4, 1777, Washington launched a bold four-column dawn assault on the British camp at Germantown, PA, briefly driving Howe's forces back before the attack collapsed in confusion, fog, and a costly detour to reduce an isolated redoubt in the Chew House. Though a tactical failure, the battle convinced French observers that the Americans could mount complex offensive operations, helping tip France toward open alliance with the United States. The Germantown battlefield is now within the densely populated northwest Philadelphia neighborhood of Germantown, with Cliveden (the Chew House) a National Trust for Historic Preservation site open to the public. No detecting is possible in the urban landscape, but Cliveden still bears musket ball scars on its stone walls from the battle.

name: Battle of Bemis Heights (Second Saratoga)
description: On October 7, 1777, General Burgoyne sent a reconnaissance-in-force against the American left flank at Bemis Heights, NY, triggering a furious counterattack led by the brilliant but insubordinate Benedict Arnold that smashed through the British lines and captured key redoubts before nightfall. The American victory forced Burgoyne's full retreat and then surrender of his entire army of 6,000 men on October 17 — the first time a British army had surrendered in the field — convincing France to enter the war as America's ally and transforming the Revolution into a world war. Saratoga National Historical Park preserves the battlefield across rolling Hudson Valley farmland, with fortification remains, monuments, and a tour road giving access to key positions. Detecting is prohibited in the park, but the Saratoga region's private lands have yielded significant quantities of period military hardware.

name: Valley Forge Encampment
description: From December 1777 to June 1778, George Washington's Continental Army endured the legendary winter encampment at Valley Forge, PA — a crucible of suffering and transformation where approximately 2,000 soldiers died of disease, exposure, and starvation, but where Baron Friedrich von Steuben drilled the surviving force into a disciplined professional army. The months of misery and training produced the soldiers who would fight the British to a standstill at Monmouth Court House and ultimately win the war. Valley Forge National Historical Park preserves 3,500 acres of the encampment with reconstructed huts, earthworks, Washington's headquarters, and miles of interpretive trails. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but Valley Forge represents one of the most significant metal detecting sites in American history — outside park boundaries, private land in Chester and Montgomery counties has yielded Continetal buttons, pewter spoons, musket balls, and countless camp artifacts from the six-month occupation.

name: Battle of Monmouth
description: On June 28, 1778, Washington's army intercepted the British column retreating across New Jersey from Philadelphia to New York at Monmouth Court House, launching an attack that initially went badly when General Charles Lee ordered an unauthorized retreat — prompting Washington to famously relieve Lee on the spot and rally the army. The battle that followed was the longest single-day engagement of the Revolution, fought in brutal heat that killed as many men as the fighting; it ended essentially as a draw, with both sides holding their positions. Monmouth Battlefield State Park in Freehold, NJ, preserves over 3,000 acres of rolling farmland that remarkably retains its 18th-century appearance, with earthworks, the Craig House, and the Tennent Church still visible. Metal detecting is prohibited in the state park, but surrounding Monmouth County private lands have produced Continental and British military buttons, Brown Bess musket balls, pewter canteen fragments, and numerous camp items.

name: Battle of Rhode Island
description: In August 1778, the first Franco-American combined military operation unfolded when General John Sullivan's 10,000-man army attempted to drive the British from Newport, RI, supported by the French fleet under d'Estaing. When the French fleet was damaged by a storm and withdrew to Boston for repairs, Sullivan was left alone and had to withdraw his army from Aquidneck Island after a rear-guard action at Quaker Hill on August 29 that repulsed British pursuit. The battlefield on the northern end of Aquidneck Island is now largely within the town of Portsmouth, RI, with the Butts Hill Fort earthworks — the main American defensive position — surviving as a local historic site. The battle area offers limited private land access, but the broader Rhode Island campaign zone has produced period military hardware and camp debris.

name: Battle of Kings Mountain
description: On October 7, 1780, a force of Patriot 'Overmountain Men' — frontier militia from Virginia, North Carolina, and what would become Tennessee — surrounded and destroyed a Loyalist force under Major Patrick Ferguson on the wooded summit of Kings Mountain, SC, in a battle of about an hour that killed Ferguson and eliminated the left wing of Cornwallis's army. The victory shattered British plans for a Loyalist pacification of the Carolinas and forced Cornwallis to abandon his invasion of North Carolina, a turning point in the Southern Campaign. Kings Mountain National Military Park near Blacksburg, SC, preserves the battlefield with trails around the ridge and Ferguson's grave. Detecting is prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding piedmont farmland of York County, SC, saw extensive Patriot and Loyalist raids throughout the war and private land searches have yielded period buttons, musket balls, and accoutrements.

name: Battle of Camden
description: On August 16, 1780, British General Cornwallis's army shattered General Horatio Gates's hastily assembled force of Continentals and militia at Camden, SC, in one of the worst American defeats of the Revolution, killing or capturing over 2,000 men and destroying the Southern Army as an effective force. Gates himself fled the field on one of the fastest horses in the army, leaving his troops to their fate — a humiliation that ended his military career. Historic Camden Revolutionary War Site preserves portions of the British fortified town and the broader battle area, with ongoing archaeology. Metal detecting is prohibited at the preserved sites, but the broad Kershaw County landscape north of Camden, scene of the approach march and the rout, has yielded British regimental buttons, musket balls, and period camp debris on private land.

name: Battle of Springfield
description: On June 23, 1780, a British force of 5,000 men under General Wilhelm von Knyphausen launched a second invasion of New Jersey, advancing on Springfield and burning much of the town in a battle that was intended to draw Washington's army out of the Watchung Mountains for a decisive engagement. New Jersey Continentals and militia under General Nathanael Greene fought a tenacious delaying action, and the British eventually withdrew — never again invading New Jersey in force. The town of Springfield, NJ, was largely rebuilt after the burning and is now a modern suburb, with a small marker at the Old First Church and a monument noting the battle. The broader short hills and Watchung Mountain corridor saw extensive Continental encampment and fortification activity, and private property in the region has yielded Continental and British military artifacts.

name: Battle of Cowpens
description: On January 17, 1781, Brigadier General Daniel Morgan executed a masterful tactical double envelopment at Cowpens, SC, using a deliberately planned three-line defense to draw the British cavalry charge, then having the militia feign retreat before the Continentals and militia turned together to destroy Banastre Tarleton's feared Legion — killing, wounding, or capturing over 900 British troops. The victory was one of the most tactically brilliant of the entire war and effectively crippled Cornwallis's light forces, contributing directly to the Yorktown campaign. Cowpens National Battlefield near Gaffney, SC, preserves the rolling piedmont terrain with an auto tour, walking trail, and visitor center marking Morgan's positions. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Cherokee County farmland in the piedmont of South Carolina saw Patriot and Loyalist operations throughout the war, with private land yielding period musket balls, buttons, and accoutrements.

name: Siege of Ninety-Six
description: In May and June 1781, General Nathanael Greene besieged the Loyalist-held Star Fort at Ninety Six, SC — a key backcountry fortification garrisoned by New Jersey Loyalists — in the longest siege of the Southern Campaign. Though a relief column under Rawdon forced Greene to abandon the siege before the fort fell, the British subsequently abandoned Ninety Six as untenable, effectively ending Loyalist power in the South Carolina backcountry. Ninety Six National Historic Site in Ninety Six, SC, preserves the remarkably intact Star Fort earthworks and the siege approaches in an open field setting with excellent interpretive trails. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding Greenwood County area has yielded period buttons, musket balls, and camp artifacts from the extensive military operations in this region.

name: Battle of Guilford Court House
description: On March 15, 1781, General Nathanael Greene's army fought Cornwallis's regulars in the forests and fields around Guilford Court House, NC, in a brutal three-line engagement that inflicted approximately 530 British casualties — over 25% of Cornwallis's force — though the British technically held the field. The pyrrhic British 'victory' left Cornwallis unable to continue campaigning in the Carolinas and prompted his march to Virginia that ended at Yorktown. Guilford Courthouse National Military Park in Greensboro, NC, is one of the better preserved Southern Campaign sites, with monuments, earthworks traces, and a visitor center. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Guilford County farmland has produced period relics on private property, and the site remains one of the most archaeologically productive Southern Campaign battlefields.

name: Battle of Hobkirk's Hill
description: On April 25, 1781, General Nathanael Greene's Continental Army attacked a smaller British force under Lord Rawdon at Hobkirk's Hill near Camden, SC, but the assault fell apart when a Continental regiment broke ranks in response to a British flanking maneuver, turning a near-victory into an unnecessary defeat. Despite the tactical setback, Greene's campaign in the Carolinas was succeeding strategically — the British were being forced back toward the coast even when they won on the battlefield. The battle site is now largely within modern Camden, SC, with the Historic Camden Revolutionary War Site nearby preserving portions of the fortified town. The Camden area remains one of the better hunting grounds in the Southeast for private land searches, with the surrounding Kershaw County farmland having yielded numerous period artifacts.

name: Battle of Eutaw Springs
description: On September 8, 1781, the last major battle in the Carolinas was fought at Eutaw Springs, SC, when General Greene's army attacked a British force under Colonel Stewart, initially routing the British camp before the assault dissolved as hungry American troops stopped to plunder the British food stores. The battle was tactically inconclusive, but the British retreated to Charleston and never again ventured into the South Carolina interior, effectively ending British presence outside the coastal cities. The Eutaw Springs battlefield near Eutawville, SC, is now beneath Lake Marion — the Santee Cooper reservoir built in 1941 flooded the site. The flooding makes this a unique underwater battlefield of the Revolution, inaccessible to conventional metal detecting but a poignant reminder of lost history.

name: Battle of Green Spring
description: On July 6, 1781, Anthony Wayne's Pennsylvania Continentals were lured into an ambush by Cornwallis's army near Green Spring Plantation in Virginia, after Cornwallis feigned a river crossing to deceive the pursuing Americans into thinking he had already crossed the James River. Wayne escaped destruction by boldly charging forward with his vastly outnumbered force in a calculated risk that created confusion and allowed most of his command to withdraw. The battle site near Williamsburg, VA, is partially preserved as part of the Colonial National Historical Park system, though much of the terrain has been absorbed into the James City County area. The tidewater Virginia region saw extensive 1781 campaign movements, and private property along Cornwallis's march route has produced period military hardware.

name: Siege of Yorktown
description: From September to October 1781, General Washington and French General Rochambeau besieged Cornwallis's 8,000-man British army in its fortifications at Yorktown, VA, in the decisive operation of the Revolutionary War, with the French fleet under de Grasse blocking rescue by sea. After parallel siege approaches and the storming of key redoubts on October 14 — including a famous charge by Alexander Hamilton — Cornwallis surrendered his army on October 19, effectively ending major combat operations of the Revolution. Colonial National Historical Park at Yorktown, VA, preserves extensive earthworks, redoubt remains, and the surrender field, with ongoing archaeology revealing remarkable artifact concentrations. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding York County peninsula saw the passage of thousands of troops and has produced significant finds on private property including Minié balls from the later Civil War occupation as well as 18th-century artifacts.

name: Battle of Groton Heights
description: On September 6, 1781, the same day Benedict Arnold burned New London, CT, a British force under Colonel Edmund Eyre attacked Fort Griswold across the Thames River in Groton, where 150 militia under Colonel William Ledyard defended the small fortification against 800 British regulars. After a fierce defense, Ledyard surrendered the fort but was immediately killed with his own sword; the British then massacred much of the garrison, killing 85 men in one of the most notorious war crimes of the Revolution. Fort Griswold Battlefield State Park in Groton, CT, preserves the fort earthworks, the monument to the fallen, and a state museum on the site of the massacre. Detecting is not permitted in the state park, but the Thames River valley and surrounding New London County have yielded period musket balls and military hardware on private land.

name: Battle of Blue Licks
description: On August 19, 1782, the last major Revolutionary War battle east of the Mississippi was fought at the Blue Licks salt springs crossing in Kentucky, where a pursuing force of Kentucky militia was ambushed by Wyandot, Shawnee, and Loyalist rangers, killing 70 men including Daniel Boone's son Israel in a disastrous rout. The defeat at Blue Licks came months after Yorktown, as frontier warfare in Kentucky continued beyond the formal end of the eastern war. Blue Licks Battlefield State Resort Park near Mount Olivet, KY, preserves the crossing and rolling meadow terrain of the fight, with a monument to the fallen and an interpretive museum. The surrounding Nicholas County countryside has produced period musket balls and frontier-era hardware on private land, and the salt lick itself attracted trade and travel for generations.

name: Battle of Cowan's Ford
description: On February 1, 1781, Cornwallis's army crossed the Catawba River at Cowan's Ford in present-day Huntersville, NC, driving off a defending force of North Carolina militia under General William Davidson, who was killed in the crossing. The British fording of the rain-swollen Catawba was a daring feat that kept up the pressure on Greene's retreating army as Cornwallis raced northward in pursuit. The battle site at the Catawba River is now largely within the Duke Power / Lake Norman reservoir area north of Charlotte, with a small marker near McDowell Nature Center. The broader Piedmont North Carolina region saw Cornwallis's army march across private farmland for weeks in early 1781, and the route has produced period British military buttons, musket balls, and horseshoe hardware on private land.

name: Battle of Wetzell's Mill
description: On March 6, 1781, the British cavalry under Tarleton and Cornwallis's light infantry clashed with an American rear guard under Colonel Otho Williams at Wetzell's Mill on Reedy Fork Creek near Guilford Court House, NC, as Greene continued his famous retreat northward. The American rear guard fought effectively and withdrew in good order, and the engagement was one of several skirmishes during Greene's legendary 'Race to the Dan' that preserved his army from destruction. The battle site near Gibsonville, NC, is private farmland in Guilford County, largely unchanged from the 1781 landscape. This area of the Piedmont saw intensive maneuvering by both armies and private land metal detecting with permission has produced period buttons, musket balls, and horse equipment.

name: Battle of Tippecanoe
description: On November 7, 1811, General William Henry Harrison led 1,000 U.S. Army regulars and militia against the confederacy of Native American tribes assembled by the Shawnee leader Tecumseh and his brother the Prophet at Prophetstown along the Tippecanoe River in Indiana Territory. A predawn attack by the warriors caught Harrison's camp by surprise, but the Americans repulsed the assault and destroyed the village, breaking the confederacy's military power in the Old Northwest — though Tecumseh was away rallying tribes in the South. The Tippecanoe Battlefield site is preserved as a Tippecanoe County park near Battle Ground, IN, with monuments, a museum, and an interpretive trail through the historic terrain. Metal detecting is prohibited in the park, but the surrounding Lafayette area farmland saw extensive military passage and encampment activity throughout the War of 1812 period, and private land searches have yielded early 19th-century military buttons and musket balls.

name: Battle of Mackinac Island
description: In July 1812, a British and Indian force from Fort St. Joseph captured Fort Mackinac on Mackinac Island, Michigan, in the opening weeks of the War of 1812 — the American garrison awakened to find their fort surrounded and had no choice but to surrender without a shot fired. The island's strategic position controlling the straits between Lake Huron and Lake Michigan made it one of the most hotly contested points of the war, with Americans failing in two attempts to retake it. Fort Mackinac is today one of Michigan's finest historic sites, with its original stone walls and buildings intact on the bluff above the Victorian resort community on Mackinac Island. The island forbids motorized vehicles, and metal detecting is prohibited in the historic fort and state park areas, though the island's grounds have yielded British and American military artifacts during archaeological work.

name: Siege of Fort Detroit
description: In August 1812, British General Isaac Brock led a combined British-Canadian-Indian force including Tecumseh's warriors across the Detroit River and bluffed American General William Hull into surrendering the entire garrison of Fort Detroit and the Michigan Territory without a fight, in one of the most stunning capitulations in American military history. Hull's cowardly surrender of 2,500 men shocked the nation and allowed Britain to consolidate control of the upper Great Lakes for much of the war. The site of Fort Detroit is now within the downtown core of modern Detroit, with a small historical marker; the original fort was demolished long ago. The broader Detroit River corridor and private lands across the Detroit metropolitan area saw extensive military operations during the war, though urban development has covered most of the landscape.

name: Battle of Queenston Heights
description: On October 13, 1812, American forces crossed the Niagara River and seized the commanding heights above Queenston, Ontario, in what should have been a significant early victory — but British General Isaac Brock was killed leading a counterattack, and a second British and Indian assault eventually drove the Americans back across the river with heavy losses when New York militia refused to cross into Canada. Brock's death turned a British tactical success into a national tragedy for Canada, and he became the defining hero of the War of 1812 in Canadian memory. Queenston Heights Battlefield Park in Queenston, Ontario, preserves the heights with Brock's monument — a soaring column visible across the Niagara Gorge — and trails marking the key positions. The Niagara frontier saw some of the war's most intensive fighting and private land in the region has produced War of 1812 military buttons, musket balls, and belt plates.

name: Battle of Frenchtown (River Raisin)
description: On January 22, 1813, a British and Indian force under Henry Procter and Roundhead attacked and defeated an American force under General Winchester at Frenchtown on the River Raisin in present-day Monroe, MI, killing or capturing over 400 men. The following day, Indian warriors killed between 30 and 100 American wounded who had been left behind — the 'River Raisin Massacre' — generating the war cry 'Remember the Raisin!' that drove American enlistment for the rest of the war. River Raisin National Battlefield Park in Monroe, MI, is one of the nation's newest national battlefields (authorized 2009), with a visitor center and interpretation of the January 1813 fighting. Metal detecting is prohibited within the park, but surrounding Monroe County farmland along the River Raisin corridor has yielded period musket balls, military buttons, and battle debris on private land.

name: Battle of York (Toronto)
description: On April 27, 1813, an American amphibious force of 1,700 soldiers under General Zebulon Pike and Commodore Chauncey attacked and captured York (present-day Toronto), the capital of Upper Canada, in one of the war's most significant American victories. Pike was killed when the retreating British garrison detonated the fort's powder magazine — an explosion that also wounded hundreds of soldiers on both sides — and the Americans burned the Parliament buildings before withdrawing. The battle site is now within the heart of downtown Toronto, near the grounds of the Ontario Legislature at Queen's Park; Fort York National Historic Site preserves a collection of original 1812-era blockhouses and barracks from the subsequent British refortification. The Toronto waterfront and park system cover most of the battlefield, with no detecting opportunities, though the original lakeshore has produced period artifacts during archaeological excavations.

name: Battle of Fort George
description: On May 27, 1813, a large American amphibious assault at the mouth of the Niagara River successfully drove the British garrison from Fort George, Ontario, opening the Canadian side of the Niagara frontier to American occupation for several months. The assault, supported by naval gunfire from Chauncey's lake fleet, was one of the best-executed American operations of the war, and the British retreated inland to regroup. Fort George National Historic Site in Niagara-on-the-Lake, Ontario, is one of Canada's finest military heritage sites, with meticulously restored earthworks, barracks, and a magazine from the original fortification. No detecting is allowed in the national historic site, but the Niagara frontier remains a highly productive region for private land searches, with War of 1812 buttons, musket balls, and camp debris documented from farms along the river corridor.

name: Battle of Sackett's Harbor
description: On May 29, 1813, a British fleet from Kingston transported 750 regulars and marines under Sir George Prevost to attack the crucial American naval base at Sackett's Harbor, NY, the main American shipyard on Lake Ontario. American militia and regulars under General Jacob Brown repulsed the landing after fierce fighting, and the British withdrew — a significant American defensive success that protected the naval construction program that would give the U.S. control of Lake Ontario by war's end. Sackett's Harbor Battlefield State Historic Site in Sackett's Harbor, NY, preserves the earthworks and the Madison Barracks complex overlooking Black River Bay. Detecting is not permitted at the state historic site, but the surrounding Jefferson County shores and private lands have yielded War of 1812-era military buttons, musket balls, and naval hardware.

name: Battle of Stoney Creek
description: On June 6, 1813, a daring British night attack of 700 soldiers under Colonel John Harvey surprised the American camp at Stoney Creek near Hamilton, Ontario, capturing two American generals (Chandler and Winder) in the confusion and forcing an American withdrawal from their advanced position toward Niagara. The victory helped stabilize the British position on the Niagara peninsula and demonstrated that the smaller British force could use bold tactics to offset American numerical superiority. Battlefield House Museum and Park in Stoney Creek (now part of Hamilton), Ontario, preserves the Nash farmhouse used as a British field hospital and overlooks the battlefield terrain. The Hamilton area's private farmland has produced War of 1812 musket balls, buttons, and military hardware from the intense 1813 Niagara campaign.

name: Battle of Beaver Dams
description: On June 24, 1813, a party of 500 American soldiers under Lieutenant Colonel Charles Boerstler marching to destroy a British outpost at Beaver Dams, Ontario, was ambushed by Mohawk and Caughnawaga warriors who had been warned of the movement — according to legend, by the heroic Laura Secord's 20-mile warning walk. The warriors surrounded and harassed the American column for hours until a small British force arrived and the exhausted Americans surrendered. The battle site near Thorold, Ontario, is commemorated by a monument and small park at the Decew House area. The surrounding Niagara Peninsula farmland has produced War of 1812 military buttons, musket balls, and equipment from the intense 1813 campaign on private land with permission.

name: Battle of Lake Erie
description: On September 10, 1813, Master Commandant Oliver Hazard Perry won a decisive naval battle against a British squadron on Lake Erie at Put-in-Bay, Ohio, immortalized by his after-action report: 'We have met the enemy and they are ours.' The American victory gave the U.S. control of Lake Erie, cut British supply lines to the west, and enabled Harrison's invasion of Canada that would kill Tecumseh at the Thames. The battle site on Lake Erie near the Bass Islands, Ohio, is commemorated by Perry's Victory and International Peace Memorial on South Bass Island (Put-in-Bay) — a 352-foot Doric column and national monument. The surrounding Bass Islands and Erie shoreline are accessible by ferry, and the lake bed in the battle area (mostly in Canadian waters) represents an underwater archaeological site of significance.

name: Battle of the Thames
description: On October 5, 1813, General William Henry Harrison's army crossed into Upper Canada and caught the retreating British and Indian forces under General Procter at the Thames River (Moraviantown, Ontario), routing the British in minutes and killing the great Shawnee confederacy leader Tecumseh in the melee — ending the most serious Native American military resistance in the eastern half of North America. The death of Tecumseh broke the Western Confederacy and secured American dominance in the Great Lakes region. The battle site at Moraviantown (Thamesville), Ontario, is marked by a monument and local historic site near Chatham-Kent. The surrounding Thames River valley farmland in Ontario has produced period military buttons, musket balls, and related artifacts from the 1813 campaign on private land.

name: Battle of Châteauguay
description: On October 26, 1813, a Canadian force of fewer than 500 men — primarily French-Canadian Voltigeurs under Lieutenant Colonel Charles de Salaberry — repulsed an American army of 4,000 under General Wade Hampton at the Châteauguay River south of Montreal, using the forested terrain and disciplined fire to create the impression of a much larger defending force. The victory saved Montreal from capture and is one of the most celebrated engagements in Canadian military history, celebrated as proof that French Canadians were defenders of their homeland. The Châteauguay battlefield is preserved as a national historic site in Allan's Corners, Quebec, with a monument and interpretive panels. The surrounding Châteauguay Valley farmland has produced period musket balls, military buttons, and camp debris from both the American approach and the engagement on private land.

name: Battle of Crysler's Farm
description: On November 11, 1813, a British force of 800 regulars and Canadian militia under Lieutenant Colonel Joseph Morrison defeated over 2,000 Americans under General John Boyd at Crysler's Farm on the St. Lawrence River near present-day Morrisburg, Ontario, ending the American attempt to capture Montreal via the river route. The British regulars used close-order fire discipline to repeatedly repulse the disordered American attacks, demonstrating the superiority of professional troops over militia in open battle. The original Crysler's Farm site was flooded by the St. Lawrence Seaway construction in 1958, but the battle is commemorated at Upper Canada Village and the Crysler's Farm Battlefield Memorial in Morrisburg, Ontario. The flooding makes this a unique underwater battlefield, and the surrounding shoreline area has produced period artifacts during low-water periods and dredging operations.

name: Battle of Horseshoe Bend
description: On March 27, 1814, General Andrew Jackson led 3,000 Tennessee militia and Cherokee allies against approximately 1,000 Red Stick Creek warriors fortified in a horseshoe-shaped bend of the Tallapoosa River in Alabama, killing approximately 800 warriors in the most decisive engagement of the Creek War. The victory broke the military power of the Red Stick faction and forced the Creek Nation to cede 23 million acres of territory in the Treaty of Fort Jackson, opening much of Alabama and Georgia to white settlement. Horseshoe Bend National Military Park near Dadeville, AL, preserves the battlefield peninsula with trails, an auto tour, and a museum. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Tallapoosa County private land along the river has produced War of 1812-era musket balls, arrowheads, and military equipment.

name: Battle of Lundy's Lane
description: On July 25, 1814, the bloodiest battle of the War of 1812 was fought at Lundy's Lane just yards from Niagara Falls, as British and American forces fought a brutal, close-range nighttime battle for a hill and its artillery that neither side could clearly see. Both armies suffered enormous casualties — roughly 1,700 combined — and the Americans withdrew to Fort Erie despite claiming a tactical victory, effectively ending offensive American operations on the Niagara frontier. The battlefield at Drummond Hill Cemetery in Niagara Falls, Ontario, contains the burial mound of soldiers killed in the battle and is maintained as a historic site. The Niagara Falls area is heavily developed, but the broader Niagara frontier private farmland corridor has yielded War of 1812 musket balls, military buttons, belt plates, and canteen fragments.

name: Battle of Bladensburg
description: On August 24, 1814, British General Robert Ross's veterans routed an American force of 6,000 — including President Madison and his cabinet watching from a nearby hill — at Bladensburg, MD, in what became known as the 'Bladensburg Races' for the speed of the American flight. The defeat opened the road to Washington, and that night the British burned the Capitol, the White House, and other public buildings in retaliation for the American burning of York. Bladensburg Waterfront Park and the Bladensburg battlefield in Prince George's County, MD, are within the modern Washington metropolitan area; a small state park preserves a portion of the field. The surrounding Maryland suburbs have occasional private land access where the approach routes have yielded period military buttons and musket balls.

name: Burning of Washington
description: On the night of August 24-25, 1814, following their victory at Bladensburg, British forces under Admiral Cockburn and General Ross marched into Washington and burned the Capitol building, the White House, the Treasury, and the War Department in the only successful foreign capture of the U.S. capital — though a fortuitous rainstorm limited the destruction. The burning was ostensibly in retaliation for the American burning of York (Toronto) the previous year, though it also served as a demonstration of British military power. The sites of the burned buildings are now within the core of modern Washington, D.C., rebuilt and expanded through the 19th and 20th centuries with the White House and Capitol still occupying their original locations. No detecting is possible in the federal capital, but the British march routes through Maryland and Virginia have produced period military artifacts on private land.

name: Battle of Fort McHenry
description: From September 13-14, 1814, a British fleet bombarded Fort McHenry at the entrance to Baltimore Harbor for 25 hours — the attack that inspired Francis Scott Key to write what would become 'The Star-Spangled Banner' — but the fort's garrison under Major George Armistead held firm, and the British fleet withdrew without capturing the city. The failed attack on Baltimore, combined with the American naval victory at Plattsburgh the same week, contributed to British willingness to negotiate the peace that ended the war. Fort McHenry National Monument and Historic Shrine in Baltimore, MD, preserves the star-shaped fort with its original earthworks in excellent condition, a national monument with a moving interpretive program. Metal detecting is prohibited on the national monument grounds, but the Baltimore Harbor area and surrounding private land in Baltimore County saw extensive military operations and encampment activity during the 1814 campaign.

name: Battle of Plattsburgh
description: On September 11, 1814, the most strategically important American victory of the War of 1812 was won at Plattsburgh, NY, when Commodore Thomas Macdonough's fleet defeated a superior British naval squadron on Lake Champlain just as British General Prevost's 11,000-man army began to advance, prompting Prevost to abort his invasion of New York and retreat to Canada. The dual victory — on land and water — fatally weakened Britain's bargaining position at Ghent and led directly to a peace treaty that restored prewar boundaries. The battlefield and naval action site at Plattsburgh, NY, are commemorated at the Plattsburgh Battle Monument and the Thomas Macdonough Monument, with the Cumberland Bay shoreline marking the naval battle zone. Private land along the Lake Champlain shore and the Saranac River corridor has yielded War of 1812-era military buttons, musket balls, and naval hardware.

name: Battle of New Orleans
description: On January 8, 1815, General Andrew Jackson's mixed force of regular troops, Kentucky and Tennessee militia, free Black soldiers, Choctaw warriors, and Jean Lafitte's pirates repulsed massed British assaults at the Rodriguez Canal below New Orleans, killing or wounding approximately 2,000 British soldiers including their commanding general Pakenham while suffering only about 70 casualties — the most lopsided victory in American military history. The battle, fought two weeks after the peace treaty had been signed in Ghent (news traveled slowly), made Jackson a national hero and the foundation of his path to the presidency. Chalmette Battlefield, part of Jean Lafitte National Historical Park near New Orleans, LA, preserves the battlefield with the Rodriguez Canal earthworks and the Chalmette Monument. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but the broader St. Bernard Parish area along the Mississippi River has yielded period musket balls, rifle balls, and military hardware on private land.

name: Battle of Fort Meigs
description: In the spring of 1813, British General Procter and Tecumseh besieged Fort Meigs on the Maumee River in present-day Perrysburg, OH — the largest wooden fort in America — twice trying to capture the key American supply base for Harrison's Northwestern Army but failing both times. The first siege in May saw a relief column cut up in an ambush, but the fort itself held; the second attempt in July was repulsed when Tecumseh's warriors drove off the besiegers. Fort Meigs State Memorial in Perrysburg, OH, is a remarkable reconstruction of the massive wooden fortification on its original site, with extensive period artillery and interpretive programs. Detecting is not permitted at the state memorial, but the Maumee River valley private farmland has produced War of 1812-era military hardware and encampment debris.

name: Battle of Lake Champlain
description: The naval Battle of Lake Champlain on September 11, 1814, was fought simultaneously with the land battle at Plattsburgh, NY, when Commodore Thomas Macdonough's American squadron — anchored in Plattsburgh Bay with springs on the anchor cables to allow rotation — destroyed the British fleet under Captain George Downie. Downie was killed in the opening minutes, and after three hours of close-range fighting, all four major British warships had surrendered or been sunk, leaving the lake entirely in American control. The sunken British fleet in Plattsburgh Bay represents significant submerged cultural resources — the wreck of HMS Linnet and other vessels have been located by maritime archaeologists. The lake bed is protected from disturbance, but the shoreline areas of Clinton and Essex counties, NY, saw extensive military encampment and naval activity during the 1814 campaign.

name: Battle of Conjocta Creek
description: On August 3, 1814, a British force advancing from Fort George attempted to attack the American position at Lewiston, NY, but was repulsed at Conjocta Creek (now Scajaquada Creek) in Buffalo by American militia and regulars under General Peter Porter. The skirmish was part of the intense fighting along the Niagara frontier in the summer of 1814 as both sides probed each other's defenses following the major engagements at Chippawa and Lundy's Lane. The creek now flows through Buffalo's Delaware Park and the Buffalo Museum of Science area, with the battle site entirely within the modern urban environment. The broader Niagara frontier private farmland corridor has produced War of 1812 musket balls, military buttons, and camp artifacts from the summer 1814 campaign.

name: Battle of Chippawa
description: On July 5, 1814, General Jacob Brown's Army of the Niagara defeated British regulars under General Riall at the Chippawa River in Ontario in a battle that demonstrated — for the first time in the war — that American regulars could match British professionals in open-field combat. Riall is reputed to have exclaimed 'Those are regulars, by God!' when he saw the Americans advance steadily under fire, a line that entered American military mythology. The battle site on the Chippawa River in Niagara Falls, Ontario, is marked by monuments and a small park; Chippawa Battlefield Park preserves a portion of the field. The surrounding Welland County farmland has yielded War of 1812 military buttons, musket balls, and horseshoe hardware from the 1814 campaign on private land.

name: Battle of Palo Alto
description: On May 8, 1846, General Zachary Taylor's 2,300-man Army of Occupation defeated a larger Mexican army under General Mariano Arista at Palo Alto, TX, in the first major engagement of the Mexican-American War, using superior American artillery — firing exploding shells that set the tall prairie grass afire — to rout the Mexican formations. The battle was one of the first to demonstrate the decisive effect of modern artillery in open field combat, and Taylor's aggressive advance on Mexican soil set the political stage for the declaration of war. Palo Alto Battlefield National Historical Park near Brownsville, TX, preserves the original prairie terrain with interpretive trails and a visitor center, though much of the site was threatened by development before its protection. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Cameron County private land has yielded period musket balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the 1846 campaign.

name: Battle of Resaca de la Palma
description: The day after Palo Alto, on May 9, 1846, Taylor pursued Arista's retreating Mexican army and encountered their new position in the dense chaparral along the Resaca de la Palma — a dry oxbow lake channel — where American cavalry under Captain Charles May charged and captured the Mexican artillery, resulting in a rout that cost Arista's force thousands of casualties and prisoners as his army fled across the Rio Grande. The twin victories of Palo Alto and Resaca de la Palma secured Taylor's hold on the north bank of the Rio Grande and opened the Mexican-American War. The battle site is within and adjacent to Palo Alto Battlefield National Historical Park and the urban sprawl of Brownsville, TX, with portions accessible at the national park. The chaparral terrain where the fighting raged has produced period Mexican and American military hardware during authorized archaeological survey work.

name: Siege of Monterrey
description: In September 1846, General Zachary Taylor besieged and captured the fortified Mexican city of Monterrey, Nuevo León, in three days of intense urban street fighting — one of the largest and most complex military operations in American history to that point. American soldiers cleared buildings block by block using picks and crowbars to break through walls rather than expose themselves in the streets, while artillery pounded the Mexican fortifications. The city of Monterrey is today a major industrial metropolis in northern Mexico, with the historic district and the Bishop's Palace (El Obispado, scene of fierce fighting) preserved as cultural sites. The palace and the city center saw the heaviest fighting, and period military artifacts have been found during construction and renovation work in the historic core.

name: Battle of Buena Vista
description: On February 22-23, 1847, General Zachary Taylor's 4,800-man force — mostly volunteers — fought off Santa Anna's 15,000-man army at the mountain pass of Buena Vista near Saltillo, Coahuila, in two days of desperate fighting that included a famous cavalry charge repulsed by the Mississippi Rifles under Jefferson Davis. The American victory, won against three-to-one odds, made Taylor a national hero and effectively ended Mexican operations in northern Mexico. The battlefield site in the high desert of Coahuila, Mexico, is marked by a memorial near the hacienda of Buena Vista; the rugged terrain is largely unchanged. Period military artifacts including American and Mexican uniform buttons, musket balls, and equipment have been found in the surrounding area.

name: Siege of Veracruz
description: In March 1847, General Winfield Scott landed 12,000 American troops south of Veracruz in the largest amphibious operation in American history up to that point, then besieged the fortified city and castle of San Juan de Ulúa, pounding them into surrender in 18 days with a massive artillery bombardment. The fall of Veracruz opened Scott's Aztec Club march toward Mexico City along Cortés's ancient route, and the campaign that followed proved a masterclass in strategic maneuver. The port city of Veracruz on Mexico's Gulf coast is a major modern city, with the historic fortress of San Juan de Ulúa preserved on its island in the harbor as a museum. The harbor area and surrounding Veracruz state have preserved period artifacts in archaeological contexts.

name: Battle of Cerro Gordo
description: On April 17-18, 1847, General Scott's army flanked the seemingly impregnable Mexican position at Cerro Gordo pass in the Sierra Madres, with Captain Robert E. Lee discovering a goat path through the mountains that allowed American forces to appear in the Mexican rear, routing Santa Anna's army and capturing 3,000 prisoners and his coach (and reportedly his cork leg). The battle was a textbook example of operational maneuver that Scott's subordinates — including Lee, Grant, and McClellan — would remember when they commanded their own armies in the Civil War. The pass at Cerro Gordo in Veracruz state, Mexico, is marked by a monument and the terrain is largely open hillside. Period Mexican and American military artifacts have been found in the area during agricultural and construction work.

name: Battle of Contreras
description: On August 19-20, 1847, during Scott's final approach to Mexico City, American forces fought a two-stage battle at Contreras (Padierna), where the first day saw General Gideon Pillow's troops pinned by Mexican artillery, and the second day saw a brilliant 500-man flanking column under Brigadier General Persifor Smith execute a lightning 17-minute attack at dawn that routed the Mexican position. The victory at Contreras opened the road to Churubusco and ultimately Mexico City. The battle site is now within the southern suburban sprawl of Mexico City, the terrain largely buried under modern development. This campaign produced a generation of officers who would command the armies of both sides in the Civil War.

name: Battle of Churubusco
description: On August 20, 1847, immediately following Contreras, Scott's army fought another fierce engagement at the fortified convent of San Pablo Churubusco south of Mexico City, where the Mexican garrison including the San Patricio Battalion — Irish-American deserters fighting for Mexico — made a desperate defense before being overwhelmed. The San Patricio prisoners taken at Churubusco were later hanged for desertion in one of the most controversial episodes of the war. The convent church at Churubusco is now within the Churubusco neighborhood of Mexico City, preserved as the Museo Nacional de las Intervenciones, a national museum on the site of the battle. The broader Mexico City region was the climax of the 1847 campaign, and archaeological work in the urban core has recovered period military artifacts.

name: Battle of Molino del Rey
description: On September 8, 1847, General Worth's division attacked the Molino del Rey foundry complex outside Chapultepec Castle on the outskirts of Mexico City, believing the Mexicans were casting cannon there — the assault proved costly and the foundry less important than hoped, with American casualties of nearly 800 men in an attack considered by many historians to be unnecessary. The battle remains one of the bloodiest engagements of the Mexican-American War relative to the numbers engaged, with over 25% American casualties. The site of Molino del Rey is now within the Bosque de Chapultepec (Chapultepec Forest), Mexico City's great urban park. Archaeological investigation in the park has recovered period artifacts, and the castle of Chapultepec overlooking the site is a national museum.

name: Battle of Chapultepec
description: On September 13, 1847, American forces stormed the Military Academy atop Chapultepec Hill — the 'Halls of Montezuma' celebrated in the Marine Corps Hymn — in the penultimate battle before the fall of Mexico City, with American troops scaling the 200-foot hill under fire and fighting hand-to-hand with the Mexican cadets defending the castle. The defense by the young Niños Héroes cadets who refused to surrender became one of the most honored sacrifices in Mexican national memory. Chapultepec Castle atop the hill in Mexico City's Bosque de Chapultepec is today the Museo Nacional de Historia and a UNESCO World Heritage site. The hill and surroundings saw the war's heaviest fighting in its final days, and period artifacts have been recovered during renovation and archaeological work.

name: Battle of El Brazito
description: On December 25, 1846, Colonel Alexander Doniphan's Missouri Volunteers defeated a Mexican force at El Brazito on the Rio Grande in southern New Mexico, in a Christmas Day battle that opened the road to El Paso del Norte (present-day Juárez/El Paso). Doniphan's remarkable Expedition — a 3,600-mile march from Fort Leavenworth to Mexico City — is one of the longest military marches in American history. The battle site is near present-day La Mesa, NM, largely within the agricultural Rio Grande valley that is now part of the greater El Paso metropolitan area. The broad Mesilla Valley saw extensive military traffic from the 1846-48 war and the later Civil War, and private land searches have yielded period military hardware.

name: Battle of Sacramento
description: On February 28, 1847, Doniphan's 1,000-man Missouri volunteer force defeated a 4,000-man Mexican army strongly positioned behind artillery at the Sacramento River crossing north of Chihuahua City, in a brilliantly improvised flanking attack that demonstrated the fighting qualities of American frontier volunteers.
