description: On July 28, 1864, Hood launched his third offensive in eight days — an attack on the Union Fifteenth Corps moving to cut the last Confederate rail line into Atlanta at Ezra Church, GA — only to be repulsed with over 3,000 casualties against minimal Union losses. The battle demonstrated that Hood's aggressive strategy was bleeding the Army of Tennessee white without achieving strategic goals. The Ezra Church battlefield is now within the western Atlanta metropolitan area in the Mozley Park neighborhood. Civil War Minié balls and artifacts have been found in the area during development, but the dense urban landscape limits modern detecting opportunities.

name: Battle of Jonesborough
description: On August 31 - September 1, 1864, Sherman's forces cut the last remaining Confederate rail line into Atlanta at Jonesborough, GA, forcing Hood to evacuate the city rather than be cut off — Atlanta fell to Sherman on September 2, 1864, in one of the war's most consequential moments, helping ensure Lincoln's reelection. The Battle of Jonesborough was the final engagement of the four-month Atlanta Campaign. The Jonesborough battlefield in Clayton County, GA, is marked by the Road to Tara Museum in Historic Jonesborough and various Civil War Trails markers around the town. Surrounding Clayton County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the August 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Mobile Bay
description: On August 5, 1864, Admiral David Farragut led his fleet through the heavily mined (torpedoed) entrance to Mobile Bay, AL, uttering (or improvising the reported words) 'Damn the torpedoes, full speed ahead!' when the ironclad Tecumseh struck a mine and sank. The Union fleet destroyed the Confederate defending ships and captured Fort Morgan after a three-week siege, closing Mobile to blockade runners. Fort Morgan State Historic Site at the tip of the Baldwin County peninsula, AL, preserves the original masonry fortification in excellent condition, with the Civil War earthworks overlaying the original masonry. Metal detecting is not permitted at the state historic site, but the Gulf of Mexico off Fort Morgan is a productive dive site with wreck artifacts, and surrounding Baldwin County private land has produced Civil War relics.

name: Battle of Second Deep Bottom
description: On August 14-20, 1864, Grant launched a combined infantry and cavalry operation at Deep Bottom on the James River north of Richmond to draw Confederate reserves away from the Petersburg front — the action was part of Grant's strategy of keeping Lee's entire line under constant pressure. Despite capturing some ground initially, the operation failed to penetrate the Richmond defenses. The Deep Bottom site in Henrico County, VA, is preserved as part of Richmond National Battlefield Park's Deep Bottom unit. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private land in the James River corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the numerous 1864 operations in this area.

name: Battle of Globe Tavern
description: On August 18-21, 1864, Union forces under General Gouverneur Warren seized the Weldon Railroad at Globe Tavern (Yellow House Tavern) south of Petersburg, VA, cutting one of the last railroads supplying the Confederate city — Confederate counterattacks briefly drove the Federals back but ultimately failed to dislodge them from the critical rail line. The Globe Tavern action was one of several engagements in the Siege of Petersburg that progressively strangled Confederate supply lines. The battlefield at Globe Tavern in Dinwiddie County, VA, is partially preserved within the Petersburg National Battlefield, with other portions on private farmland. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park units, but the surrounding private land in Dinwiddie County has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Reams Station
description: On August 25, 1864, Confederate forces under A.P. Hill attacked Union troops at Reams Station on the Weldon Railroad in Dinwiddie County, VA, routing two Union Second Corps divisions whose veterans were exhausted and demoralized after months of hard fighting, capturing 2,700 men and 9 artillery pieces. The defeat was a rare setback for Grant's operations and demonstrated the resilience of the Confederate army even at this late stage of the war. The Reams Station battlefield in Dinwiddie County, VA, is partially preserved with Civil War Trails markers; the terrain is largely private farmland and second-growth forest. Surrounding private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the intense August 1864 operations along the Weldon Railroad.

name: Battle of Berryville
description: On September 3, 1864, Union cavalry under General Wesley Merritt attacked a Confederate infantry column from Early's army at Berryville, VA, in a fight that demonstrated the continued presence of Confederate forces in the lower Shenandoah Valley even as Sheridan prepared his major offensive. The engagement was a prelude to the decisive Third Battle of Winchester two weeks later. The Berryville battlefield in Clarke County, VA, is largely private farmland in the lower Shenandoah Valley. The Valley corridor from Winchester to Berryville saw intense military operations throughout 1862-1865, and private land in Clarke County has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and cavalry relics.

name: Third Battle of Winchester (Opequon)
description: On September 19, 1864, General Philip Sheridan launched his campaign to clear the Shenandoah Valley with a powerful assault on Early's Confederate forces at the Third Battle of Winchester (Battle of Opequon), VA, driving the Confederates through the city in a victory that gave the Union firm control of the northern valley. The battle was the largest Civil War engagement in the Shenandoah Valley, with over 15,000 combined casualties. Winchester, VA, changed hands more than 70 times during the war, and the area saw more sustained military activity than almost any region in America. The Shenandoah Valley Battlefields Foundation and the Civil War Trust have preserved significant portions of all three Winchester battlefields, with private land in the surrounding Frederick County area producing significant quantities of Civil War relics.

name: Battle of Fisher's Hill
description: On September 22, 1864, three days after Winchester, Sheridan attacked Early's new defensive position at Fisher's Hill near Strasburg, VA — a ridgeline the Confederates considered impregnable — by sending General George Crook's corps on a concealed flank march through Massanutten Mountain's timber while the front was attacked. The Confederate line collapsed, sending Early's army fleeing southward up the Valley. Fisher's Hill Battlefield in Shenandoah County, VA, is preserved by the Civil War Trust and open to the public, with hiking trails through the excellent earthwork remains. Surrounding Shenandoah County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from Sheridan's 1864 Valley Campaign.

name: Battle of Cedar Creek
description: On October 19, 1864, Confederate General Jubal Early launched a surprise dawn attack on Sheridan's encamped Army of the Shenandoah at Cedar Creek near Strasburg, VA, shattering two Union corps before the advance stalled as soldiers plundered the captured camps. Sheridan — famously 20 miles away in Winchester — rode to the battlefield and personally rallied his army for a counterattack that routed the Confederates and ended Southern offensive operations in the Shenandoah Valley. Cedar Creek and Belle Grove National Historical Park in Shenandoah County, VA, preserves the Belle Grove Plantation and portions of the battlefield with excellent walking tours. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private farmland in the Cedar Creek corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the October 1864 battle.

name: Battle of Allatoona Pass
description: On October 5, 1864, Confederate General Samuel French attacked a small Union garrison defending the vital railroad supply depot at Allatoona Pass, GA, in an attempt to cut Sherman's supply line as Hood began his campaign northward through Georgia. The garrison under General John Corse held off the attackers for hours until relief forces appeared on the distant signal flags — the battle inspired the famous hymn 'Hold the Fort.' Allatoona Pass battlefield in Bartow County, GA, is preserved by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and Georgia DNR, with trails through the dramatic natural pass terrain and original earthwork remains. Metal detecting is not permitted in the preserved areas, but surrounding Bartow County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the October 1864 engagement.

name: Siege of Petersburg
description: From June 1864 to April 1865, Grant's Army of the Potomac and Lee's Army of Northern Virginia faced each other across 37 miles of earthworks at the Siege of Petersburg, VA — the longest sustained siege in American history — while Grant used his numerical superiority to extend the Confederate lines ever westward until Lee's thinning defenses finally broke. The 293-day siege featured mining operations (culminating in the Battle of the Crater), repeated flank extensions, and constant pressure that ultimately wore down the Confederate army. Petersburg National Battlefield preserves portions of the extensive siege lines south and east of Petersburg, including the Crater site and City Point (Grant's headquarters). Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but the enormous siege perimeter — much of which crosses private land — has been exceptionally productive, with Minié balls, artillery fragments, CS and US belt plates, buttons, and camp debris documented throughout the area.

name: Battle of the Crater
description: On July 30, 1864, Pennsylvania coal miners in the Union army tunneled under the Confederate lines at Petersburg, VA, and detonated four tons of black powder in the largest man-made explosion in North American history to that point — but the ensuing assault was mismanaged, with troops crowding into the crater rather than going around it, and Confederate counterattacks eventually retook the ground with heavy casualties including many Black Union troops. The failed attack cost the Union over 3,700 casualties and wasted an extraordinary tactical opportunity. The Crater site is preserved at Petersburg National Battlefield's eastern front units in Petersburg, VA, with a trail descending to the depression still visible in the ground. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding Dinwiddie County private land has produced significant Civil War relics from the siege operations.

name: Battle of Second Reams Station
description: The second engagement at Reams Station on August 25, 1864, saw Confederate General A.P. Hill attack Union troops tearing up the Weldon Railroad, routing two Union Second Corps divisions that broke and fled despite numerical superiority — a morale failure Grant attributed to the exhaustion of veteran units after months of continuous combat. The defeat temporarily set back Union operations along the railroad south of Petersburg. The second Reams Station battlefield site in Dinwiddie County, VA, overlaps with the first engagement location and is largely private agricultural land. The broader Weldon Railroad corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls, railroad equipment, and military hardware from the intense operations of August 1864 on private land.

name: Battle of Five Forks
description: On April 1, 1865, General Sheridan and the Army of the Potomac crushed the Confederate force defending the Five Forks road junction in Dinwiddie County, VA — Pickett's division was away at a 'shad bake' fish fry when the attack struck, and the Confederate right flank was shattered, forcing Lee to evacuate Richmond and Petersburg the following night. Five Forks was the decisive engagement that ended the siege of Petersburg and effectively sealed the Confederacy's fate. Five Forks Battlefield, part of Petersburg National Battlefield, preserves the road junction and earthwork remains in Dinwiddie County, VA, with interpretive trails. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private land in Dinwiddie County has produced April 1865 relics including Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Nashville
description: On December 15-16, 1864, General George Thomas's Army of the Cumberland delivered one of the most complete tactical victories of the Civil War, utterly destroying Hood's Army of Tennessee in a two-day battle at Nashville, TN — the Confederate army collapsed completely on the second day, with units simply dissolving and men streaming southward. The battle effectively ended Confederate military operations in the western theater. Nashville is a major modern city, and the battlefield terrain is largely within the metropolitan area — some earthwork fragments survive, with Tennessee Civil War National Heritage Area interpretation available. The Fort Negley and Fort Houston earthwork positions in Nashville are preserved as city parks, and private land on Nashville's outskirts has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware.

name: Battle of Franklin
description: On November 30, 1864, in one of the war's most tragic engagements, Confederate General John Bell Hood ordered a frontal assault of 18,000 men across two miles of open ground against Union entrenchments at Franklin, TN — the charge killed six Confederate generals (more than any other battle), including the brilliant Patrick Cleburne, and inflicted horrific losses on the Army of Tennessee without breaking the Union position. The Union army withdrew that night, but the Confederate force had been effectively destroyed as an offensive weapon. The Carter House and Carnton Plantation in Franklin, TN, are preserved as private museums at the core of the battlefield, with the Confederate cemetery at Carnton containing over 1,500 soldier graves. Metal detecting is not permitted at the historic sites, but surrounding Williamson County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, belt plates, and artillery fragments.

name: Battle of Spring Hill
description: On November 29, 1864, the day before Franklin, the entire Union Army of the Ohio slipped past Hood's Confederate army at Spring Hill, TN, in one of the war's great controversies — somehow, through a combination of darkness, miscommunication, and confusion, Schofield's entire force marched within earshot of Confederate camps and escaped northward to Franklin. Hood was furious and blamed his subordinates for what became known as 'the Spring Hill affair.' The Spring Hill battlefield in Maury County, TN, is partially preserved at the Thompson-Walker House and along Saturn Parkway, with the town of Spring Hill having grown substantially around the battle area. Surrounding private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the November 1864 Tennessee Campaign.

name: Battle of Saltville
description: On October 2, 1864, Union General Stephen Burbridge led a raid to destroy the vital Confederate salt works at Saltville, VA — a critical Confederate supply source — but was repulsed by Confederate defenders. The battle was followed by a massacre of wounded Black Union soldiers left on the field, a war crime attributed to Confederate forces including Champ Ferguson. Saltville, VA, in Smyth County preserves the historic salt works site and a small museum commemorating the battle. The surrounding Smyth County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware, and the town's salt heritage museum provides context for the strategic importance of the site.

name: Battle of Tom's Brook
description: On October 9, 1864, Sheridan's cavalry under General Alfred Torbert achieved a decisive victory over Confederate cavalry under Rosser and Lomax at Tom's Brook in Shenandoah County, VA, routing both Confederate cavalry divisions and pursuing them for 26 miles — a rout so complete that Union troopers called it the 'Woodstock Races' after the pursued Confederates fled through Woodstock. The battle stripped Early's army of effective cavalry protection and contributed to the disaster at Cedar Creek ten days later. The Tom's Brook battlefield along U.S. Route 11 in Shenandoah County, VA, is largely private farmland with Civil War Trails markers. Surrounding private land in the Valley corridor has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls.

name: Battle of Darbytown Road
description: On October 7, 1864, Confederate General Richard Anderson's forces attacked Union positions along the Darbytown Road north of the James River in Henrico County, VA, in one of several Confederate attempts to retake the ground lost at Chaffin's Farm and New Market Heights in late September. The attack was repulsed by Union forces including Black troops. The Darbytown Road battlefield is within the modern Richmond and Henrico County metropolitan area, largely developed. The broad Richmond perimeter north of the James has been productive for Civil War relic hunters on private land with permission, with Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware found throughout the area.

name: Battle of Hatcher's Run
description: On February 5-7, 1865, Grant extended his left flank at Hatcher's Run in Dinwiddie County, VA, in a major operation that failed to reach the Boydton Plank Road but succeeded in stretching the Confederate defensive line to its breaking point and establishing new siege positions. The battle was one of the last major engagements before the final breakthrough at Five Forks. The Hatcher's Run battlefield is partially preserved within Petersburg National Battlefield's western units in Dinwiddie County, VA, with earthwork traces visible in the second-growth forest. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the late-war Petersburg operations.

name: Battle of Waynesboro
description: On March 2, 1865, Sheridan's cavalry struck the remnant of Early's Confederate Valley Army at Waynesboro, VA, capturing over 1,600 men and virtually all of Early's artillery in a brief engagement that ended Confederate organized resistance in the Shenandoah Valley. Early escaped with a handful of officers, and the Shenandoah Valley was effectively cleared. Waynesboro, VA, in Augusta County is a modern small city; the battlefield is within the town and surrounding private farmland. The Shenandoah Valley private farmland in Augusta County has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the March 1865 operation and earlier Valley campaigns.

name: Battle of Bentonville
description: On March 19-21, 1865, Confederate General Joseph Johnston launched the last major Confederate offensive in the eastern theater at Bentonville, NC, attacking a portion of Sherman's March to the Sea army before the Union forces concentrated and counterattacked — Johnston's force was ultimately too small to achieve a decisive result and was forced to withdraw, ending any hope of stopping Sherman's advance to join Grant. Bentonville Battlefield State Historic Site in Johnston County, NC, preserves over 1,600 acres of the battle terrain with the Harper House (used as a field hospital) and excellent walking trails through intact earthworks. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but surrounding Johnston County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from Sherman's March.

name: Battle of Averasborough
description: On March 16, 1865, Confederate General William Hardee's corps fought a delaying action at Averasborough, NC, against Sherman's left wing — buying Johnston time to concentrate his forces for the larger engagement at Bentonville three days later. The battle was one of the last significant Confederate offensive actions in the Carolinas. The Averasborough battlefield in Harnett County, NC, is preserved by a local foundation with interpretive trails near Dunn. Surrounding Harnett County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the March 1865 Carolina Campaign.

name: Battle of Fort Stedman
description: On March 25, 1865, Lee ordered his last major offensive of the war — a dawn attack by General Gordon's corps on Fort Stedman in the Union siege lines east of Petersburg, VA — breaking through briefly before Union reserves counterattacked and sealed the breach, inflicting 4,000 Confederate casualties the Petersburg garrison could not replace. The defeat accelerated the Confederate collapse that would come just days later. Fort Stedman is preserved as part of Petersburg National Battlefield in Petersburg, VA, with the earthwork positions clearly visible on the tour drive. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Dinwiddie County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the final phase of the Petersburg siege.

name: Battle of Sayler's Creek
description: On April 6, 1865, in the largest engagement of the Appomattox Campaign, Union forces overtook portions of Lee's retreating army at Sayler's Creek (Sailor's Creek), VA, cutting off two entire Confederate corps and capturing nearly 8,000 men — including eight generals. The disaster at Sayler's Creek prompted Lee's famous lament: 'My God, has the army dissolved?' Three days later he surrendered at Appomattox. Sayler's Creek Battlefield State Historical Park in Amelia and Prince Edward counties, VA, preserves the Hillsman and Overton-Hillsman farmhouses and the creek terrain. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding private land along the Appomattox Campaign route has produced April 1865 Minié balls, buttons, and military hardware.

name: Battle of High Bridge
description: On April 6-7, 1865, retreating Confederate forces burned most of the massive High Bridge railroad trestle over the Appomattox River in Prince Edward County, VA, while Union cavalry fought to prevent the destruction, galloping across the lower wagon bridge to attack the Confederate rear guard. The bridge was one of the engineering marvels of Virginia, and its partial destruction slowed but did not stop the Union pursuit. High Bridge Trail State Park in Prince Edward County, VA, features the restored bridge (now a rail-trail pedestrian bridge) 125 feet above the Appomattox River, a stunning historic landmark in an otherwise rural setting. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Prince Edward County private farmland along the Appomattox River has produced April 1865 Civil War relics.

name: Battle of Appomattox Court House
description: On April 9, 1865, the Army of Northern Virginia — reduced to perhaps 28,000 exhausted men — made its final attempt to break through the Union lines at Appomattox Court House, VA, before Lee sent out the white flag. Grant and Lee met in the parlor of Wilmer McLean's house to sign the terms of surrender that effectively ended the Civil War, four years after the first shots at Fort Sumter. Appomattox Court House National Historical Park preserves the entire village with the McLean House reconstruction, the original brick courthouse, and the surrender field in rural Appomattox County, VA. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but the surrounding private farmland along the retreat route from Petersburg has produced April 1865 Minié balls, discarded equipment, and military hardware — relics of Lee's last desperate march.

name: Battle of Mobile (Spanish Fort)
description: In March-April 1865, Union General E.R.S. Canby besieged Spanish Fort and Fort Blakely on the eastern shore of Mobile Bay, Alabama — two Confederate strongpoints protecting the city of Mobile. After a three-week siege, Confederate forces evacuated Spanish Fort on April 8, and Fort Blakely fell the following day in a massive Union assault, securing the final surrender of Mobile. The Spanish Fort and Fort Blakely sites in Baldwin County, AL, are partially preserved with earthwork remains; Blakely State Park maintains some of the most intact Civil War earthworks in the South, with hundreds of yards of preserved Confederate defensive lines. Metal detecting is not permitted in Blakely State Park, but the surrounding Baldwin County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Selma
description: On April 2, 1865, Union cavalry under General James Wilson captured the Confederate industrial city of Selma, AL — home to the Selma Arsenal, foundries, and naval works that had supplied the Confederacy — in a battle where Wilson's troopers broke through a six-mile defensive perimeter and overwhelmed the outnumbered Confederate defenders under General Nathan Bedford Forrest. The fall of Selma destroyed the last major Confederate industrial capacity in the Deep South. Selma is a historic city on the Alabama River, and the battlefield terrain is largely within the modern city, with Old Live Oak Cemetery and various historical markers noting the engagement. The broader Dallas County area has produced Civil War cavalry relics on private land from Wilson's Raid through Alabama in the spring of 1865.

name: Battle of Columbus (Georgia)
description: On April 16, 1865 — one week after Lee's surrender at Appomattox — Union cavalry under General James Wilson attacked and captured Columbus, GA, in the last significant land battle of the Civil War east of the Mississippi, destroying the Confederate naval works, textile mills, and munitions facilities in the city. Confederate Brigadier General Reuben Wright's force made a spirited defense before being overwhelmed at midnight. Columbus, GA, is a modern city on the Chattahoochee River, and the battlefield is within the city limits, with a Heritage Walk and various historical markers noting the final battle's locations. The broader Chattahoochee Valley corridor has produced Civil War cavalry relics on private land.

name: Second Battle of Corinth
description: On October 3-4, 1862, Confederate Generals Van Dorn and Price attacked the Union garrison at Corinth, MS, in a determined two-day assault that broke through the outer Union lines on October 3 before being repulsed with devastating losses on October 4 — Battery Robinett, defended by the 11th Missouri Infantry, was the scene of some of the most intense fighting. The Confederate defeat ended organized Southern resistance in northern Mississippi. The Corinth Civil War Interpretive Center in Corinth, MS, is an NPS site at the national cemetery interpreting the 1862 siege and battle; Battery Robinett earthworks are preserved nearby. Surrounding Alcorn County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from both the 1862 siege and battle.

name: Battle of Tupelo
description: On July 14-15, 1864, Union General Andrew Smith's force defeated Nathan Bedford Forrest's Confederate cavalry and supporting infantry at Tupelo (Harrisburg), MS, in a battle specifically designed to keep Forrest occupied and away from Sherman's supply lines during the Atlanta Campaign. Though Smith ultimately withdrew back to Memphis, the battle achieved its strategic goal of neutralizing Forrest for the crucial summer weeks. Tupelo National Battlefield is a small NPS unit in Tupelo, MS, preserving a portion of the battlefield terrain along Highway 6 near the city. Surrounding Lee County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and cavalry relics from Forrest's operations in northeastern Mississippi.

name: Battle of New Bern
description: On March 14, 1862, General Ambrose Burnside's amphibious force of 11,000 men landed at Slocum's Creek and attacked the Confederate defensive line south of New Bern, NC, routing the defenders and capturing the city in a battle that gave the Union a major foothold on the North Carolina coast. New Bern remained in Union hands for the rest of the war, serving as a major logistical base for coastal operations. New Bern, NC, is a well-preserved historic city at the confluence of the Neuse and Trent rivers, with numerous antebellum buildings and the New Bern Battlefield Park preserving a portion of the 1862 defensive positions. Metal detecting is not permitted in the preserved battlefield areas, but surrounding Craven County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Roanoke Island
description: On February 7-8, 1862, General Burnside and Flag Officer Goldsborough captured Roanoke Island, NC, the Confederate stronghold guarding Croatan Sound, with an amphibious assault that overwhelmed the island's defenders and opened the Albemarle Sound to Union naval operations. The fall of Roanoke Island enabled Burnside's subsequent operations against New Bern, Fort Macon, and the North Carolina coastal plain. The Roanoke Island Freedmen's Colony historical site and Fort Huger earthworks preserve portions of this largely forgotten but strategically vital 1862 operation. Dare County's barrier island landscape retains much of its 19th-century character, and the island's beaches and interior have yielded period artifacts during archaeological surveys.

name: Battle of Drewry's Bluff
description: On May 15, 1862, Confederate cannon at Drewry's Bluff on the James River below Richmond, VA, stopped a Union naval flotilla including the famous Monitor from steaming up to attack the Confederate capital — the high bluff presented firing angles the Monitor's guns couldn't match, and after an hour the Federal gunboats withdrew. The fort at Drewry's Bluff was a critical Confederate position throughout the war, also serving as a prisoner of war facility. Drewry's Bluff, now Fort Darling, is part of Richmond National Battlefield Park in Chesterfield County, VA, with the fort earthworks, gun emplacements, and the James River overlook well preserved. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private land along the James River corridor has produced Civil War naval and fort relics.

name: Battle of Secessionville
description: On June 16, 1862, Union forces under General Henry Benham launched an unauthorized predawn assault on Confederate Tower Battery (Fort Lamar) at Secessionville on James Island, SC, but were repulsed by a smaller Confederate garrison in a costly frontal attack that cost 683 Union casualties against 204 Confederate. The defeat ended Union operations toward Charleston from James Island for the time being. The Secessionville battlefield on James Island, SC (now part of suburban Charleston), has been partially preserved by the Secessionville Historical Society. Metal detecting is not permitted on preserved portions, but this area of the Sea Islands and Charleston Harbor has been historically productive for Civil War coastal fortification relics.

name: Battle of Olustee
description: On February 20, 1864, Confederate forces under General Joseph Finegan ambushed a Union column advancing from Jacksonville, FL, at Olustee (Ocean Pond) in the pine flatwoods of Baker County, routing the Federals and inflicting over 1,800 casualties in the bloodiest battle ever fought in Florida. Black Union regiments including the 54th Massachusetts and 8th USCT fought with particular distinction during the retreat. Olustee Battlefield Historic State Park near Olustee, FL, preserves the pine flatwood terrain with the original earthwork traces and an annual battle reenactment. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Baker County private pineland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from this decisive Florida engagement.

name: Battle of Natural Bridge
description: On March 6, 1865, Confederate forces including Florida militia, cadets from the West Florida Seminary (Florida State University's predecessor), and regular troops repulsed a Union landing force attempting to cross the St. Marks River at Natural Bridge — the last Confederate defensive victory in the Deep South. The victory prevented the capture of Tallahassee, the only Confederate capital east of the Mississippi never occupied by Union forces. Natural Bridge Battlefield Historic State Park near Woodville, FL, preserves the crossing terrain with earthwork remnants and interpretive markers, holding an annual reenactment. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Leon and Wakulla County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the engagement.

name: Battle of New Market Heights
description: On September 29, 1864, thirteen Black Union soldiers earned the Medal of Honor in the assault on New Market Heights north of the James River at Richmond, VA — one of the largest single-day awards of the Medal of Honor to Black soldiers in American history. The assault by USCT (United States Colored Troops) under General David Birney broke the Confederate line after withering close-range fire, demonstrating beyond question the combat effectiveness of Black Union troops. The New Market Heights battlefield in Henrico County, VA, is preserved as a Richmond National Battlefield Park unit. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Henrico County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, USCT military hardware, and military equipment from the September 1864 Richmond operations.

name: Battle of Chaffin's Farm
description: On September 29-30, 1864, Union forces attacked the Confederate defenses north of the James River in a combined operation at Chaffin's Farm and New Market Heights, capturing Fort Harrison in one of the boldest Union tactical successes of the Petersburg Campaign and threatening the outer Richmond defenses. Confederate counterattacks on September 30 failed to retake Fort Harrison, which was held by the Union for the rest of the war as a key position in the expanding Richmond-Petersburg siege lines. Richmond National Battlefield Park's Fort Harrison unit in Henrico County, VA, preserves the earthworks with hiking trails along the extensive fortifications. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Boydton Plank Road
description: On October 27-28, 1864, Grant launched another extension of his siege lines at Petersburg, attempting to cut the Boydton Plank Road — the last Confederate supply route into the city — in a major operation involving 43,000 Union troops. Confederate forces under Hampton and Heth counterattacked and forced the Union forces back, but the operation succeeded in extending the siege lines and maintaining pressure. The Boydton Plank Road battlefield in Dinwiddie County, VA, is partially preserved within Petersburg National Battlefield's western units. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park areas, but surrounding private farmland in Dinwiddie County has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the late 1864 siege operations.

name: Battle of Honey Hill
description: On November 30, 1864, a Union force attempting to cut the railroad between Charleston and Savannah was repulsed at Honey Hill (Grahamville), SC, in a battle fought through dense Carolina lowcountry vegetation that negated Union numerical superiority. The Confederate defenders, including a small force of regulars and militia, held their earthwork position against repeated assaults. The Honey Hill battlefield near Ridgeland, SC, in Hampton County is largely private farmland and woodland in the South Carolina coastal plain. The surrounding coastal plain private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the operations supporting Sherman's March through the Carolinas.

name: Battle of Second Fredericksburg
description: The Second Battle of Fredericksburg on May 3, 1863 — part of the Chancellorsville Campaign — saw Sedgwick's Union Sixth Corps storm the famous Marye's Heights that had slaughtered thousands the previous December, this time capturing the position relatively quickly as the Confederate force was thinner and surprised by the determination of the attack. Sedgwick then moved toward Chancellorsville before being stopped at Salem Church. Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park preserves Marye's Heights, the Sunken Road and its famous stone wall, and the National Cemetery with its thousands of Union graves. Surrounding Stafford County private land has produced remarkable quantities of Civil War relics from the multiple engagements at Fredericksburg in 1862 and 1863.

name: Battle of Spotsylvania (Bloody Angle)
description: On May 12, 1864, the Union assault on the Mule Shoe salient at Spotsylvania Court House reached its climax at the 'Bloody Angle' — a jutting corner of Confederate earthworks where Union and Confederate troops fought hand-to-hand across log parapets for nearly 20 hours in pouring rain, with men pushing bayonets through the logs and using rifle butts as clubs in some of the most savage close-quarters combat of the entire war. The log breastwork at the Bloody Angle was reportedly knee-deep in bodies. Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park preserves the original earthworks of the Bloody Angle in Spotsylvania County, VA, with a walking trail along the parapets — some of the most evocative surviving Civil War fortifications in America. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited, but surrounding private land has produced extraordinary quantities of Civil War relics.

name: Battle of North Anna
description: On May 23-26, 1864, Grant's army crossed the North Anna River in central Virginia and found Lee's army in a brilliant inverted V-shaped defensive position that threatened to destroy any Union force that crossed the river in two separated columns. But Lee fell ill at this critical moment and could not coordinate the offensive stroke that might have devastated Grant's army; Grant disengaged and moved around Lee's flank once more. The North Anna battlefield in Hanover County, VA, is partially preserved at Hanover County's North Anna Battlefield Park and at Jericho Mill, with earthwork traces visible in the forest. Surrounding private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the May 1864 Overland Campaign.

name: Battle of Wilson's Raid
description: In March-April 1865, Union General James Wilson led 13,500 cavalry in a devastating raid through Alabama and Georgia — the largest cavalry operation in the history of the Western Hemisphere — destroying Selma, Montgomery, Columbus (GA), and dozens of Confederate industrial and supply facilities. Wilson's Raid effectively ended Confederate industrial capacity in the Deep South as Lee was surrendering to the north. The raid route crosses hundreds of miles through Alabama and Georgia, with historical markers in multiple communities along the way. The raid corridor has produced Civil War cavalry relics on private land throughout Alabama and Georgia, including cavalry weapons, equipment, and camp debris.

name: Battle of Resaca
description: On May 13-15, 1864, the first major pitched battle of Sherman's Atlanta Campaign was fought at Resaca, GA, when Sherman tried to envelope Johnston's army through Snake Creek Gap before Johnston slipped away to the Resaca ridgeline. Two days of heavy fighting ended with Johnston withdrawing southward to avoid being flanked. Resaca Battlefield in Gordon County, GA, is preserved by the Resaca Confederate Cemetery and the privately-held Resaca Battlefield Civil War site, with earthwork remains and an ongoing preservation effort. Metal detecting is not permitted on the preserved battlefield, but surrounding Gordon County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the opening phase of the Atlanta Campaign.

name: Battle of New Hope Church
description: On May 25-26, 1864, Sherman shifted his forces through rough terrain north of Dallas, GA, seeking to flank Johnston's Confederate army, only to encounter entrenched Confederate defenders at New Hope Church in a rainstorm — Union attacks were repulsed in brutal fighting in the densely wooded terrain. The area of New Hope Church, Dallas, and Pickett's Mill became the scene of ten days of constant skirmishing and entrenching that previewed the brutal combat of the Petersburg siege. New Hope Church battlefield in Paulding County, GA, is partially preserved by the Paulding County Historical Society. Surrounding private land in Paulding and Cherokee counties has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the ten days of Dallas-New Hope Church operations.

name: Battle of Pickett's Mill
description: On May 27, 1864, General Oliver Howard's Union corps attacked what was believed to be Johnston's exposed right flank at Pickett's Mill, GA — but the attack fell into a ravine with steep sides swept by Confederate fire, resulting in one of the Union's worst tactical defeats of the Atlanta Campaign, with 1,600 casualties against about 500 Confederate. Ambrose Bierce, who fought there, wrote a scathing account of the battle's incompetence. Pickett's Mill Battlefield Historic Site near Dallas, GA, is one of Georgia's finest preserved Civil War sites, with miles of hiking trails through intact ravine terrain and earthwork remains. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Paulding County private land has produced Civil War relics from the May 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Dallas
description: The Dallas, Georgia engagement from May 26 to June 4, 1864, was a week-long series of battles and skirmishes as Sherman's and Johnston's armies probed and entrenched along a line stretching from New Hope Church through Dallas and Pickett's Mill. Union assaults on the Confederate line at Dallas failed, and the campaign shifted back toward the railroad as both armies maneuvered through the dense north Georgia pine and hardwood forests. The Dallas battlefield area in Paulding County, GA, is partly preserved through various private and county preservation efforts. Surrounding Paulding County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the extended May-June 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Kenesaw Mountain
description: The broader Kennesaw Mountain area saw weeks of skirmishing and maneuvering during June-July 1864 before Sherman's famous frontal assault on June 27. The Kenesaw Mountain battlefield position in Cobb County, GA (the alternate spelling is used in some records), saw Johnston's Confederate army occupy and fortify the mountain's northern and southern faces as Sherman attempted to find a way through. Kennesaw Mountain National Battlefield Park near Marietta, GA, preserves over 2,900 acres with the mountain summit, Big Kennesaw and Little Kennesaw positions, and miles of hiking trails leading to extensive earthwork remains. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Cobb County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from this key Atlanta Campaign position.

name: Battle of Utoy Creek
description: On August 5-7, 1864, Union General Schofield's Army of the Ohio launched a series of attacks on the Confederate entrenchments at Utoy Creek on Atlanta's southwestern defenses, attempting to cut the last railroad into the city. The assaults were repulsed in heavy fighting, and Sherman was forced to resort to more extensive flanking maneuvers. The Utoy Creek battlefield is now entirely within the western Atlanta metropolitan area in Fulton County, GA, with the Utoy Creek area partially preserved near Ben Hill neighborhood. Civil War artifacts have been found during construction and development in the area, but conventional metal detecting is limited by urban development.

name: Grierson's Raid
description: From April 17 to May 2, 1863, Colonel Benjamin Grierson led 1,700 Union cavalrymen on a 600-mile raid from La Grange, TN, through the heart of Mississippi to Baton Rouge, LA, cutting telegraph lines, tearing up railroads, destroying Confederate supplies, and causing panic — all as a diversion supporting Grant's Vicksburg Campaign. Grierson's Raid was one of the most successful cavalry raids of the Civil War and inspired the novel and film 'The Horse Soldiers.' The raid route crosses multiple Mississippi counties from north to south, with Civil War Trails markers in various communities along the way. The corridor has produced Civil War cavalry relics on private farmland throughout central and southern Mississippi from the April-May 1863 operations.

name: Morgan's Raid into Indiana and Ohio
description: From July 2 to July 26, 1863, Confederate General John Hunt Morgan led over 2,000 cavalry on a 700-mile raid across Kentucky, Indiana, and Ohio — the deepest penetration of Confederate forces into Northern territory during the war — before being captured near Salineville, Ohio, after his command was destroyed piece by piece. Morgan's bold invasion terrorized the Indiana and Ohio countryside but achieved no lasting strategic result. Morgan's route is marked by historical markers through southern Indiana (Corydon) and across southern Ohio; the Salineville site in Columbiana County, OH, marks the surrender point. The raid corridor across Indiana and Ohio has produced period cavalry relics on private farm and ranch land, where Morgan's troopers abandoned equipment during their desperate flight.

name: Battle of Cloyd's Mountain
description: On May 9, 1864, General George Crook's Union force defeated Confederate General Albert Jenkins at Cloyd's Mountain, VA, in the opening blow of Grant's coordinated spring offensive — the victory opened the way to the vital New River Bridge and the Virginia and Tennessee Railroad. Jenkins was mortally wounded during the battle. The Cloyd's Mountain battlefield in Pulaski County, VA, is preserved by the Civil War Trust with hiking trails through the wooded mountain terrain. Surrounding Pulaski County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from this important but often overlooked engagement in the Virginia mountains.

name: Battle of New River Bridge
description: On May 10, 1864, the day after Cloyd's Mountain, Crook's Union cavalry destroyed the New River Bridge at Central Depot (Radford), VA — the vital Virginia and Tennessee Railroad span — crippling the Confederate supply line to Lee's army in the Shenandoah Valley. The destruction of the bridge forced Confederate forces to make emergency repairs under pressure. The bridge site at the New River in Radford, VA (Montgomery County), is within the modern city; the river gorge terrain is largely unchanged. The Montgomery County area along the New River has produced Civil War relics on private land from Crook's 1864 raid through southwest Virginia.

name: Battle of Piedmont
description: On June 5, 1864, Union General David Hunter's forces defeated and killed Confederate General William 'Grumble' Jones at Piedmont in Augusta County, VA, opening the Shenandoah Valley to Hunter's subsequent advance on Lexington and the destruction of VMI. Jones's defeat and death left the Valley poorly defended for weeks. The Piedmont battlefield in Augusta County, VA, is largely private farmland in the southern Shenandoah Valley near Port Republic. The Augusta County and Rockingham County corridor has produced significant Civil War relics from the multiple 1862, 1863, and 1864 operations in the central Shenandoah Valley on private land.

name: Battle of Lynchburg
description: On June 17-18, 1864, General Jubal Early's Confederate forces arrived by railroad from Richmond just in time to defend Lynchburg, VA, from Hunter's advancing Union army — the arrival of Breckinridge's corps emboldened the Confederates to counterattack, and Hunter withdrew westward over the mountains, effectively ceding the Shenandoah Valley and enabling Early's subsequent raid on Washington. The battle preserved Lynchburg — a vital Confederate supply and hospital city — for the rest of the war. The Lynchburg battlefield terrain is partially within the city limits of Lynchburg, VA, with Civil War Trails markers at key positions. Surrounding Campbell County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the June 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Monocacy
description: On July 9, 1864, Confederate General Jubal Early's invading force of 15,000 men defeated a Union scratch force of 5,000 under General Lew Wallace at the Monocacy River crossing near Frederick, MD — a tactical Confederate victory that was a strategic Union success, as Wallace's doomed stand bought Washington enough time to receive reinforcements before Early arrived at Fort Stevens. Wallace's decision to fight — knowing he would lose — is one of the war's most consequential command decisions. Monocacy National Battlefield near Frederick, MD, preserves over 1,600 acres of the battlefield with the Best Farm, Worthington Farm, and the Thomas Farm, all scene of intense fighting. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Frederick County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware.

name: Battle of Fort Stevens
description: On July 11-12, 1864, Jubal Early's Confederate forces reached the northern defenses of Washington at Fort Stevens in the Brightwood neighborhood (present-day DC), where President Abraham Lincoln came under fire from Confederate sharpshooters — the only time a sitting president has been under enemy fire in combat. The Confederates, seeing the arriving Union reinforcements, withdrew without assaulting the fort. Fort Stevens in Washington, D.C., is preserved as a small park by the National Park Service, with a portion of the earthwork parapet remaining — Lincoln reportedly stood on these very ramparts as soldiers were shot nearby. Metal detecting is not permitted in the national park or in Washington, D.C., but the approach routes through Montgomery County, MD, private land have produced Confederate relics from Early's 1864 invasion.

name: Battle of Rutherford's Farm
description: On July 20, 1864, Union cavalry under General William Averell defeated a Confederate infantry force at Rutherford's Farm near Winchester, VA, in a sharp engagement that slowed Early's army for a day following the Fort Stevens raid. The battle was a minor tactical success but demonstrated the growing aggressiveness of Union cavalry in the Shenandoah Valley. The Rutherford's Farm battlefield in Frederick County, VA, near Winchester is largely private farmland in the lower Shenandoah Valley. The Winchester area and lower Valley corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and military hardware from the multiple 1864 operations on private land.

name: Battle of Kernstown (Second)
description: On July 24, 1864, Confederate General Jubal Early routed a Union force under General George Crook at the Second Battle of Kernstown near Winchester, VA — the same ground where Jackson had been defeated in 1862 — driving the Federals back toward the Potomac River and temporarily regaining Confederate control of the lower Shenandoah Valley. The victory emboldened Early's subsequent raids into Pennsylvania. The Second Kernstown battlefield in Frederick County, VA, overlaps with the First Kernstown site from 1862; Civil War Trust has preserved portions of this terrain. Surrounding private farmland in the Winchester-Kernstown corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from multiple 1862 and 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Cool Spring
description: On July 18, 1864, Union General Horatio Wright's Sixth Corps was ambushed while crossing the Shenandoah River at Cool Spring (Castleman's Ferry), Clarke County, VA, by Confederate forces that inflicted over 400 casualties before the Union force withdrew back across the river. The engagement demonstrated the difficulty of Union offensive operations in the Valley before Sheridan's forces were consolidated. Cool Spring Battlefield in Clarke County, VA, is preserved by the Civil War Trust with excellent hiking access along the Shenandoah River corridor. Clarke County's rolling horse country private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, cavalry relics, and military hardware from the summer 1864 Shenandoah Valley operations.

name: Battle of Guard Hill
description: On August 16, 1864, a Union cavalry force under General Wesley Merritt encountered Confederate cavalry near Guard Hill (Front Royal), Warren County, VA, in a skirmish along the Shenandoah River during the early stages of Sheridan's campaign to clear the Valley. The engagement was one of several daily cavalry fights as Sheridan consolidated his Army of the Shenandoah. The Guard Hill area near Front Royal in Warren County, VA, is largely private farmland along the Shenandoah River. The Front Royal area has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls on private land from the active 1864 Valley Campaign operations.

name: Battle of Crooked Run
description: On August 16, 1864, Union cavalry under General George Custer fought Confederate cavalry at Crooked Run (near Front Royal, VA) on the same day as the Guard Hill engagement — these twin actions were part of the cavalry screening operations as both armies jockeyed for position in the northern Shenandoah Valley before the decisive Third Battle of Winchester in September. The Crooked Run area in Warren County, VA, is largely private farmland. The active cavalry operations of August 1864 left numerous relics across the lower Valley farmlands, and private land in Warren County has produced Civil War cavalry and infantry relics.

name: Battle of Winchester (First 1862)
description: On May 25, 1862, Stonewall Jackson's Valley Army struck Nathaniel Banks's Federals at Winchester, VA, driving them north through the streets and out of the city in a rout that swept Union forces all the way to the Potomac River. The victory was the dramatic climax of Jackson's Valley Campaign, threatening Washington and forcing Lincoln to divert 40,000 Union troops from McClellan's Peninsula Campaign to protect the capital. Winchester saw three Civil War battles (1862, 1863, 1864), and the battlefield preservation effort in Frederick County, VA, has protected significant portions of all three engagement sites. The broader Winchester area private farmland has produced remarkable quantities of Civil War relics from the multiple battles fought across this ground.

name: Battle of Second Winchester
description: On June 13-15, 1863, General Jubal Early's Confederate division routed the Union garrison at Winchester, VA, under General Robert Milroy as part of Lee's opening moves in the Gettysburg Campaign — Milroy refused to believe the reports of Confederate forces until it was too late, losing nearly 4,000 men captured and his entire artillery train. The defeat cleared the Shenandoah Valley for Lee's invasion of Pennsylvania. The Second Winchester battlefield in Frederick County, VA, is partially preserved by the Civil War Trust and the Shenandoah Valley Battlefields Foundation. Private land in the Winchester corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the June 1863 operations.

name: Battle of Snicker's Gap
description: On July 17-18, 1864, Union forces crossed the Shenandoah River at Snicker's Ferry below Snicker's Gap in Clarke County, VA, to engage Early's retreating Confederates, resulting in a confused action at Parker's Ford that saw the Federals driven back across the river with significant losses. The engagement was part of the Union pursuit of Early following his Fort Stevens raid. The Snicker's Gap and Snicker's Ferry area in Clarke County, VA, is private horse country farmland in the Virginia Blue Ridge foothills. The Clarke County corridor has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls on private land from the summer 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Ash Hollow (Blue Water Creek)
description: On September 3, 1855, General William Harney led 600 U.S. Army soldiers in a dawn attack on a Brulé Lakota Sioux village at Blue Water Creek (Ash Hollow) in Nebraska Territory, killing approximately 85 warriors, women, and children and capturing 70 more in retaliation for the Grattan Massacre of 1854. The battle was one of the opening engagements of the Army's sustained campaigns against the Plains Indians and demonstrated the brutal character of frontier warfare. The Blue Water Creek battlefield in Garden County, Nebraska, is near Lewellen; the Ash Hollow State Historical Park nearby preserves the wagon road history of the region. The surrounding Garden County area saw early Plains Indian conflict, and private land in the valley has produced period .58 caliber Springfield musket balls and military hardware from the 1855 engagement.

name: Battle of Little Bighorn
description: On June 25, 1876, Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer led roughly 210 men of the 7th Cavalry into a massive encampment of Lakota Sioux, Northern Cheyenne, and Arapaho warriors along the Little Bighorn River in Montana Territory, resulting in the complete annihilation of his command in what became the most famous defeat in American military history. The combined Indian force, numbering perhaps 2,000 warriors led by Sitting Bull and Crazy Horse, overwhelmed Custer's divided regiment in a battle that lasted less than an hour. The battlefield is preserved as Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument, with white marble headstones marking where each soldier fell across the rolling Montana grassland ridges. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited on the monument grounds, but archaeological surveys have recovered hundreds of .45-70 Springfield cartridge casings and Henry rifle rounds that helped reconstruct the battle's flow — the surrounding private ranch land in the Bighorn Valley saw extensive cavalry movements and camp activity.

name: Battle of the Rosebud
description: On June 17, 1876, eight days before Little Bighorn, General George Crook's column of 1,300 soldiers and 262 Crow and Shoshone scouts was attacked by approximately 1,500 Lakota and Cheyenne warriors under Crazy Horse on Rosebud Creek in Montana Territory — a fierce six-hour battle that forced Crook to retreat, effectively removing his column from the Little Bighorn campaign and allowing Custer to advance unsupported. The Battle of the Rosebud has been called one of Crazy Horse's greatest tactical achievements. The Rosebud Battlefield State Park near Kirby, Montana, preserves the battlefield terrain, one of Montana's most intact Indian Wars sites. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Big Horn and Rosebud County private ranch land has produced .45-70 Springfield and Indian-fired cartridge casings and cavalry equipment.

name: Battle of Wolf Mountains
description: On January 8, 1877, Colonel Nelson Miles's 5th Infantry regiment fought Crazy Horse's Lakota warriors in the rugged Wolf Mountains of Montana Territory in a winter battle fought in sub-zero temperatures and a blinding snowstorm. Miles's artillery broke the Indian assault, and Crazy Horse withdrew — one of the last significant actions of the Great Sioux War before the surrenders of 1877. The Wolf Mountains battlefield in Rosebud County, Montana, is remote private ranch land in the Tongue River badlands. The surrounding area has produced Indian Wars-era .45-70 Springfield cartridge casings and cavalry equipment, and the remote battlefield is largely unchanged from the January 1877 engagement.

name: Battle of Cedar Creek (Montana)
description: On October 21, 1876, Colonel Nelson Miles's 5th Infantry caught Sitting Bull's band near Cedar Creek in eastern Montana Territory, engaging in several days of negotiations and fighting before Sitting Bull's people escaped northward toward Canada. The engagement was part of Miles's aggressive winter campaign that would eventually drive Sitting Bull and his followers into Canadian exile. The Cedar Creek area in Prairie County, Montana, is remote private ranch and federal land. The eastern Montana badlands have produced Indian Wars cartridge casings and cavalry equipment on private and public land from Miles's sustained 1876-77 campaign operations.

name: Battle of Slim Buttes
description: On September 9-10, 1876, General George Crook's starving column — on the desperate 'Horsemeat March' following Little Bighorn — found and attacked a Lakota village at Slim Buttes in Dakota Territory (present-day Harding County, SD), killing the headman American Horse and capturing the village before Crazy Horse arrived with reinforcements that briefly threatened to overrun the camp. The battle was the first Union offensive success following the Little Bighorn disaster. Slim Buttes in Harding County, South Dakota, is remote private ranch land and national grassland in the northern Great Plains. The surrounding area has produced Indian Wars-era .45-70 Springfield casings and cavalry equipment in isolated finds from this remote campaign.

name: Battle of Dull Knife
description: On November 25, 1876, Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's 4th Cavalry destroyed the Northern Cheyenne village of Dull Knife (Morning Star) and Little Wolf in the Bighorn Mountains of Wyoming Territory, burning their lodges and winter food stores and seizing their horses — forcing the Northern Cheyenne into a desperate winter flight that resulted in enormous suffering. The destruction of Dull Knife's village was one of the decisive blows of the Great Sioux War campaign. The Dull Knife (Red Fork Canyon) battlefield in Johnson County, Wyoming, is remote private ranch land in the Powder River Basin. The region has produced Indian Wars .45-70 Springfield casings and cavalry equipment from Mackenzie's 1876 Powder River Expedition on private land.

name: Battle of Lame Deer
description: On May 7, 1877, Colonel Nelson Miles ambushed the last significant Lakota holdout under Chief Lame Deer at Muddy Creek (Lame Deer Creek) in Montana Territory, killing Lame Deer and his war chief Iron Star when a brief peace conference erupted into violence. The engagement effectively ended organized Lakota resistance and brought the Great Sioux War to a close. The Lame Deer battle site on Muddy Creek in Rosebud County, Montana, is near the modern Northern Cheyenne Reservation community of Lame Deer. Indian Wars .45-70 Springfield cartridge casings and cavalry equipment have been found in the region from the 1877 operations.

name: Battle of Nez Perce (Clearwater)
description: On July 11-12, 1877, the Nez Perce under Chief Looking Glass fought General Oliver Howard's 400-man force on the bluffs above the Clearwater River in Idaho Territory, holding off the Army long enough to escape eastward over the Lolo Trail in the beginning of the famous Nez Perce retreat toward Canada. The Battle of the Clearwater was one of the opening engagements of the 1,700-mile running campaign. The Clearwater Battlefield near Kamiah, Idaho, is preserved as a National Historic Landmark on private and tribal land, with Civil War Trails-style interpretive markers along Highway 12. The surrounding Idaho County area has produced Indian Wars .45-70 Springfield cartridge casings and cavalry equipment from the 1877 Nez Perce campaign.

name: Battle of Big Hole
description: On August 9-10, 1877, Colonel John Gibbon's 7th Infantry attacked the Nez Perce camp on the Big Hole River in Montana Territory at dawn, initially breaking into the sleeping camp but then being driven back and pinned down by Nez Perce warriors who counter-attacked and besieged Gibbon's force for two days. The Nez Perce lost approximately 60-90 people including many women and children, but the survivors escaped to continue their remarkable flight. Big Hole National Battlefield near Wisdom, Montana, preserves the battlefield with the original tepee pole frames still standing in the meadow, one of the most evocative battlefield landscapes in America. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national monument, but the surrounding Beaverhead County private ranch land has produced Indian Wars .45-70 Springfield cartridge casings and Winchester .44-40 rounds from the engagement.

name: Battle of Canyon Creek
description: On September 13, 1877, Colonel Samuel Sturgis's 7th Cavalry attempted to intercept the fleeing Nez Perce at Canyon Creek in Montana Territory — a canyon east of present-day Billings — but the Nez Perce rear guard held off the cavalry while the main band escaped up the canyon and onto the plains, continuing the great flight northward toward Canada. The battle demonstrated the Nez Perce's remarkable tactical skill in combining combat with movement. The Canyon Creek battlefield near Billings in Yellowstone County, Montana, is largely private ranch and agricultural land. The surrounding Billings area and Canyon Creek corridor have produced Indian Wars cartridge casings and cavalry equipment from the September 1877 campaign.

name: Battle of Bear Paw
description: In late September and early October 1877, the Nez Perce were caught 40 miles from the Canadian border at Bear Paw Mountain in Montana Territory by Colonel Nelson Miles's troops, fighting a six-day siege before Chief Joseph's famous surrender speech: 'From where the sun now stands, I will fight no more forever.' The capture of Joseph's band ended the 1,700-mile Nez Perce retreat and placed the survivors in reservation captivity far from their Idaho homeland. Bear Paw Battlefield, part of Nez Perce National Historical Park, is a beautifully preserved and interpreted site near Chinook in Blaine County, Montana, with original tepee rings, earthworks, and an evocative landscape. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Blaine County ranch land has produced Indian Wars cartridge casings and Nez Perce camp artifacts.

name: Sand Creek Massacre
description: On November 29, 1864, Colonel John Chivington led 700 Colorado volunteers in a dawn assault on Black Kettle's peaceful Cheyenne and Arapaho village at Sand Creek in the Colorado Territory, killing approximately 150 people — mostly women, children, and elderly — and mutilating the bodies in an atrocity that shocked even a war-hardened nation and inflamed the southern Plains wars for years. The massacre is now widely recognized as one of the most horrific war crimes committed against Native Americans. Sand Creek Massacre National Historic Site in Kiowa County, Colorado, is a remote but powerfully interpreted site preserving the cottonwood creek valley where the attack occurred, managed in partnership with Cheyenne and Arapaho tribes. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national site, but the surrounding Kiowa County ranches have produced period .58 caliber musket balls and military hardware from the engagement.

name: Battle of Beecher Island
description: On September 17-25, 1868, a company of 50 frontier scouts under Colonel George Forsyth was surrounded by approximately 750 Cheyenne, Sioux, and Arapaho warriors on a sandy island in the Arikaree Fork of the Republican River in Colorado Territory — the scouts held out for eight days, killing the renowned Cheyenne war chief Roman Nose and fighting off repeated charges from behind their horses' bodies until relief arrived. Lieutenant Frederick Beecher was killed in the action, giving the site its name. The Beecher Island battlefield in Yuma County, Colorado, is marked by a monument near Wray, CO, on private and public land. The surrounding northeastern Colorado ranch country has produced Indian Wars-era Spencer and Springfield cartridge casings and frontier scout equipment from the 1868 engagement.

name: Battle of Summit Springs
description: On July 11, 1869, General Eugene Carr's 5th Cavalry — guided by Buffalo Bill Cody as chief scout — surprised and destroyed the village of Cheyenne war chief Tall Bull at Summit Springs in Washington County, Colorado Territory, killing 52 warriors including Tall Bull and rescuing two white captives, breaking the power of the Cheyenne Dog Soldiers on the Republican River. The battle effectively ended Cheyenne raids on the Kansas-Colorado settlements. The Summit Springs battlefield in Washington County, Colorado, is marked by a monument and state historical marker on private ranch land in the northeastern Colorado shortgrass prairie. The surrounding Washington County area has produced Indian Wars-era cartridge casings and cavalry equipment from the 1869 campaign.

name: Battle of Adobe Walls
description: On June 27, 1874, approximately 700 Comanche, Cheyenne, and Kiowa warriors under Quanah Parker launched a dawn assault on 28 buffalo hunters at Adobe Walls in Hutchinson County, Texas — but the hunters' Sharps buffalo rifles, accurate to 1,000 yards, held off the massed cavalry charges in a battle that demonstrated the lethal precision of the buffalo hunters' weapons. One hunter, Billy Dixon, made a famous mile-plus shot that reportedly struck a warrior from the saddle. The Adobe Walls battle site near Canadian, TX, has a monument and state historical marker on private ranch land in the Texas Panhandle. The surrounding Hutchinson County private ranch land has produced Indian Wars-era Sharps .50 caliber cartridge casings, trade goods, and battle debris from the 1874 engagement — a significant but accessible private land detecting site.

name: Battle of Palo Duro Canyon
description: On September 28, 1874, Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's 4th Cavalry discovered and attacked Quanah Parker's main Comanche camp in the dramatic hidden canyon of Palo Duro in the Texas Panhandle, routing the warriors with few casualties but destroying their lodges, food, and winter supplies — then killing 1,048 captured horses to prevent their recovery. The destruction of their winter resources broke the Comanches' will to fight and ended the Red River War. Palo Duro Canyon State Park near Canyon, TX, is one of Texas's most spectacular parks, with the dramatic 800-foot canyon walls and a replica of the Red River War battlefield interpretive area. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Randall County private ranch land has produced Comanche camp artifacts and .45-70 Springfield cartridge casings from the 1874 operations.

name: Battle of Red River (Sweetwater Creek)
description: The Red River War of 1874 was a multi-column campaign by five U.S. Army columns that relentlessly pursued the Comanche, Kiowa, and Southern Cheyenne across the Texas Panhandle, engaging them in multiple small fights along Sweetwater Creek and other Red River tributaries until the tribes were exhausted and forced back to the reservation. The battle at Sweetwater Creek in Wheeler County, TX, was one of these many actions. The remote Texas Panhandle has been largely unchanged since the 1874 campaign, and the Sweetwater Creek corridor in Wheeler County, TX, is private ranch land. The Red River War campaign corridor has produced Indian Wars-era cartridge casings and military hardware on private ranch land throughout the Texas Panhandle.

name: Battle of Wichita Agency
description: On August 22, 1874, the Red River War opened with an attack by Kiowa warriors on a company of soldiers escorting a surveying party at the Wichita Agency (Anadarko) in Indian Territory (present-day Oklahoma) — warriors from the reservation attacked civilians and soldiers in broad daylight, marking the formal beginning of the Red River War. The town of Anadarko, OK, in Caddo County sits near the original agency location, now largely within the modern community. The Red River War campaign area across southwestern Oklahoma has produced Indian Wars-era cartridge casings and military hardware on private land from the 1874 operations.

name: Wounded Knee Massacre
description: On December 29, 1890, soldiers of the 7th Cavalry attempted to disarm Chief Big Foot's band of Miniconjou Lakota Sioux at Wounded Knee Creek on the Pine Ridge Reservation in South Dakota — when a deaf warrior named Black Coyote refused to surrender his rifle, a shot was fired (its origin disputed), and the soldiers opened fire with rifles and Hotchkiss mountain guns, killing approximately 250-300 men, women, and children and effectively ending the Plains Indian Wars. The massacre remains one of the most traumatic events in Native American history. Wounded Knee is on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation in Shannon County, SD; a small church cemetery marks the mass grave, and the site has become a place of pilgrimage and protest. The area is tribal land, and no outside metal detecting is permitted — the site is a deeply sacred place of mourning for the Lakota people.

name: Battle of White Bird Canyon
description: On June 17, 1877, the first battle of the Nez Perce War was fought when Captain David Perry led 99 soldiers into White Bird Canyon in Idaho Territory and was ambushed by Nez Perce warriors using the terrain expertly, killing 34 soldiers and wounding 4 in one of the Army's worst small-unit defeats of the Indian Wars. The Nez Perce had not started the fight — young warriors had killed settlers, triggering the war — but their tactical victory at White Bird Canyon showed the Army they faced a formidable opponent. White Bird Battlefield in Idaho County, Idaho, is preserved as part of Nez Perce National Historical Park, with dramatic canyon views and interpretive trails along Highway 95. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Idaho County private land has produced Indian Wars .45-70 Springfield and .45-60 Sharps cartridge casings from the 1877 campaign.

name: Battle of the Lolo Trail
description: In late July 1877, the Nez Perce under Chief Looking Glass crossed the Bitterroot Mountains on the ancient Lolo Trail into Montana Territory, where Captain Charles Rawn's small force had built a small fortification ('Fort Fizzle') to block their passage — the Nez Perce simply went around Rawn's position through the mountains while the settlers negotiated safe passage, continuing their remarkable flight. The Lolo Trail crossing in Lolo, Missoula County, Montana, is commemorated at Fort Fizzle Historical Site along U.S. Highway 12. The Lolo Trail (now a forest service road) traverses rugged Bitterroot Mountain terrain in the Clearwater and Lolo National Forests, and the surrounding area has produced Indian Wars-era artifacts near the trail corridor.

name: Battle of Camas Creek
description: On August 19-20, 1877, Nez Perce warriors raided General Howard's camp at Camas Creek (Camas Meadows) in Idaho Territory near present-day Kilgore, stealing approximately 150 mules from the Army pack train in a daring night operation that delayed Howard's pursuit for several days. The brilliant raid was one of the Nez Perce's most effective tactics in slowing the pursuing Army. The Camas Meadows battlefield in Clark County, Idaho, is now within the agricultural fields of the upper Snake River Plain near Kilgore, ID. The surrounding Clark County area has produced Indian Wars-era cartridge casings from the 1877 Nez Perce campaign on private land.

name: Fetterman Fight
description: On December 21, 1866, Captain William Fetterman led exactly 80 men from Fort Phil Kearny along the Bozeman Trail in Wyoming Territory — reportedly boasting he could ride through the entire Sioux nation with 80 men — and was lured into a perfectly executed ambush by Red Cloud's warriors led by Crazy Horse acting as decoy, resulting in the complete annihilation of the command in what was the worst U.S. Army defeat on the Plains until Little Bighorn a decade later. The disaster forced the Army to close the Bozeman Trail forts and sign the Fort Laramie Treaty of 1868. The Fetterman Fight site in Johnson County, Wyoming, is preserved as Fetterman Battle Site — a small state historic site near Story, WY, with a monument and interpretive markers on the ridge where the soldiers died. Metal detecting is not permitted at the state site, but surrounding Johnson County private ranch land has produced Indian Wars .58 caliber Springfield and .50 caliber cartridge casings, arrowheads, and military hardware.

name: Wagon Box Fight
description: On August 2, 1867, a small company of 32 soldiers under Captain James Powell were attacked by over 1,000 Sioux warriors near Fort Phil Kearny, Wyoming Territory — but instead of dying as at Fetterman, the soldiers had just received new breech-loading Springfield rifles capable of rapid fire, and the unprecedented volume of fire repulsed repeated charges with enormous warrior casualties. The Wagon Box Fight demonstrated that new repeating technology had fundamentally changed the balance between soldiers and warriors in open terrain. The Wagon Box Fight site near Story in Johnson County, Wyoming, has a state historical marker; the immediate area is largely private ranch land. The Johnson County area has produced Indian Wars-era cartridge casings, arrowheads, and military hardware from the intense 1866-68 Powder River country operations.

name: Hayfield Fight
description: On August 1, 1867 — the day before the Wagon Box Fight — a mowing party and their military escort near Fort C.F. Smith in Montana Territory fought off a major Crow and Sioux attack from behind their wagon barricades using new breech-loading rifles, inflicting severe casualties on the warriors. The twin victories at Hayfield and Wagon Box demonstrated to Red Cloud's coalition that the new Springfield repeaters had changed the tactical equation. Fort C.F. Smith was on the Bighorn River near present-day Fort Smith in Big Horn County, Montana; the site is on private and federal land. The surrounding Big Horn County ranch country has produced Indian Wars cartridge casings and military hardware from the 1867 Big Horn campaigns.

name: Battle of Platte Bridge Station
description: On July 26, 1865, a large Cheyenne, Sioux, and Arapaho force attacked the Platte Bridge Station (near present-day Casper, WY), ambushing Lieutenant Caspar Collins and a small relief column with approximately 3,000 warriors — Collins was killed, and the Sergeant Custard's wagon train escort was wiped out nearby. The battle was one of the most significant Plains Indian victories of 1865. The battle site in Natrona County, Wyoming, near Casper, is commemorated by Fort Caspar Museum and Historic Site on the grounds of the reconstructed fort. Metal detecting is not permitted at the historic site, but surrounding Natrona County private land has produced Indian Wars-era cartridge casings and military hardware from the Platte Bridge operations.

name: Battle of Killdeer Mountain
description: On July 28, 1864, General Alfred Sully led 2,200 soldiers against a large encampment of Hunkpapa, Sioux, and allied tribes at Killdeer Mountain in Dakota Territory (present-day North Dakota), driving them from their fortified positions in the badlands with artillery.
