The battle was part of Sully's punitive expedition following the Dakota War of 1862. Killdeer Mountain Battlefield State Historic Site in Dunn County, North Dakota, preserves the dramatic butte terrain with interpretive trails and a monument. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but surrounding Dunn County private ranch land has produced Indian Wars-era .58 caliber Springfield musket balls and military hardware from Sully's 1864 expedition.

name: Battle of Whitestone Hill
description: On September 3-5, 1863, General Alfred Sully's forces attacked a large Sioux encampment at Whitestone Hill in Dakota Territory (present-day Dickey County, ND), killing approximately 100-150 warriors and capturing 156 in a controversial attack on a camp that included many peaceful bands. The battle sparked intense debate about the indiscriminate nature of Plains Indian warfare. Whitestone Hill State Historic Site in Dickey County, North Dakota, preserves the battlefield with monuments and interpretive markers. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but surrounding Dickey County private farmland has produced Indian Wars-era military hardware and musket balls from the 1863 Dakota Expedition.

name: Battle of Wood Lake
description: On September 23, 1862, the decisive engagement of the Dakota War ended when Colonel Henry Sibley's force defeated Little Crow's warriors at Wood Lake in Yellow Medicine County, Minnesota, after the warriors' pre-dawn ambush was prematurely triggered. The victory ended major combat operations of the Dakota War, leading to the surrender of hundreds of Dakota and the infamous mass execution of 38 warriors at Mankato — the largest mass execution in American history. Wood Lake Battle Site in Yellow Medicine County, Minnesota, is a state historic site with a monument and interpretive markers in the prairie lake country of southwestern Minnesota. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but surrounding Yellow Medicine County private farmland has produced Civil War-era musket balls and military hardware from the 1862 Minnesota Dakota War.

name: Battle of Birch Coulee
description: On September 2-3, 1862, a detachment of Minnesota volunteers under Captain Hiram Grant was surrounded by Dakota warriors at Birch Coulee while burying the dead from earlier massacres, and was besieged for 31 hours before Colonel Sibley's relief column arrived — losing 23 men killed and 60 wounded in one of the costliest engagements of the Dakota War relative to numbers engaged. Birch Coulee Battlefield State Historic Site in Renville County, Minnesota, preserves the defensive perimeter used by the besieged soldiers in a rural prairie setting. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but surrounding Renville County private farmland has produced Dakota War-era musket balls and military hardware.

name: Battle of New Ulm
description: On August 19-23, 1862, the German immigrant community of New Ulm, Minnesota, repulsed two major Dakota Sioux attacks during the Dakota War, with civilian defenders and arriving militia fighting off warriors throughout the town and ultimately burning buildings to create fields of fire. The town was largely destroyed, and survivors were evacuated before the Dakota War ended. New Ulm, MN, in Brown County is a well-preserved German-American historic community; the Battle of New Ulm Museum and Schell's Brewery (used as a refuge during the battle) preserve the history. The Brown County area has produced Dakota War-era musket balls and period hardware on private land near the battle sites.

name: Battle of Fort Ridgely
description: On August 20-22, 1862, Dakota warriors under Little Crow attacked the undermanned Fort Ridgely on the Minnesota River in Nicollet County, the key military post in the region, twice attempting to overwhelm the garrison before being repulsed both times by cannon fire that the Dakota could not counter. The fort's survival was crucial to preventing the Dakota War from spreading further east. Fort Ridgely State Park and Historic Site in Nicollet County, Minnesota, preserves the original stone commissary building and reconstructed features of the 1862 fort. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Nicollet County private farmland has produced Dakota War-era musket balls and military hardware from the August 1862 attacks.

name: Battle of Steptoe Disaster
description: On May 17, 1858, Lieutenant Colonel Edward Steptoe led a column of 152 dragoons into the Palouse country of Washington Territory to mediate a dispute, only to be attacked by over 1,000 Palouse, Spokane, and Coeur d'Alene warriors who surrounded and overwhelmed the soldiers, forcing a desperate overnight retreat to safety — the Army's worst defeat in the Pacific Northwest. The subsequent punitive expedition under Colonel George Wright forced the submission of the region's tribes. The Steptoe Battlefield near Steptoe, Whitman County, Washington, is marked by a state monument on a farmland butte. Surrounding Whitman County agricultural land (the Palouse wheat-growing region) has produced period .58 caliber musket balls and military equipment from the 1858 operations.

name: Battle of Four Lakes
description: On September 1, 1858, Colonel George Wright's punitive expedition against the tribes who had defeated Steptoe fought the Battle of Four Lakes near present-day Spokane, WA, defeating a large force of Spokane, Palouse, and Coeur d'Alene warriors with disciplined infantry fire and artillery. Wright's new long-range rifles proved decisive against warriors charging on horseback. The Four Lakes battlefield is within the modern Spokane metropolitan area, largely developed. The Spokane area has produced Indian Wars-era .58 caliber Springfield rounds and military hardware from Wright's 1858 campaign during construction and development projects.

name: Battle of Spokane Plains
description: On September 5, 1858, four days after Four Lakes, Colonel George Wright's forces decisively defeated a united force of Pacific Northwest tribes at Spokane Plains near present-day Spokane, WA, breaking their military resistance and forcing submission. Wright then executed horses (confiscating over 800) and hanged tribal leaders — brutal measures that ended the Plateau Indian War. The Spokane Plains battlefield is within modern Spokane, WA, entirely developed. Wright's subsequent march through eastern Washington and Idaho resulted in the submission of multiple tribes and the permanent opening of the interior Pacific Northwest to American settlement.

name: Battle of Bear River
description: On January 29, 1863, Colonel Patrick Connor led 300 California Infantry volunteers in a dawn assault on a Shoshone village at the confluence of Beaver Creek and the Bear River in Idaho Territory (near present-day Preston, ID) — killing approximately 250-400 men, women, and children in what is now classified as a massacre and considered one of the largest slaughters of Native Americans in American history. The engagement ended Shoshone resistance to settlement of the Great Salt Lake region. The Bear River Massacre National Historic Landmark near Preston, Franklin County, Idaho, is preserved with a monument and interpretive site on private and public land. Metal detecting is not permitted at the historic site, but surrounding Franklin County farmland has produced Civil War-era musket balls and military hardware from the 1863 engagement.

name: Battle of Canyon de Chelly
description: In January 1864, Colonel Kit Carson led the U.S. Army through Canyon de Chelly in present-day Arizona — the sacred homeland of the Navajo people — capturing or starving out the Navajo who had taken refuge in the canyon's rock alcoves and forcing their surrender, which led to the Long Walk of over 9,000 Navajo to the Bosque Redondo reservation in New Mexico. Carson's campaign ended Navajo resistance and forced a traumatic four-year exile. Canyon de Chelly National Monument in the Navajo Nation of northeastern Arizona preserves the spectacular red rock canyon with 1,000-year-old cliff dwellings and is managed cooperatively with the Navajo tribe. Navajo tribal permit is required to enter the canyon beyond White House Trail; the entire area is tribal land.

name: Battle of the Washita
description: On November 27, 1868, Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer led a dawn raid on Black Kettle's Cheyenne village on the Washita River in Indian Territory (present-day Oklahoma) — the same chief who had survived Sand Creek — killing Black Kettle and approximately 103 warriors, women, and children. The surprise attack was controversial both then and now, with critics pointing to the peaceful nature of many of the village's occupants. Washita Battlefield National Historic Site near Cheyenne, OK, in Roger Mills County preserves the village site on a scenic river bend with an excellent visitor center. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national site, but surrounding Roger Mills County private land has produced Indian Wars-era .45-70 Springfield and .50 caliber cartridge casings from the 1868 engagement.

name: Marias Massacre
description: On January 23, 1870, Major Eugene Baker led four cavalry companies in a dawn attack on a peaceful Piegan Blackfoot village of Chief Heavy Runner on the Marias River in Montana Territory — killing approximately 173 Blackfoot people, mostly women and children, as the warriors were away on a hunting trip and much of the village was suffering from smallpox. The massacre was one of the most horrific and unjustified in the Indian Wars and provoked significant controversy in Washington. The Marias Massacre site near the Two Medicine River confluence in Pondera County, Montana, is on private ranch land with a small marker. Metal detecting is not permitted without landowner permission, but the surrounding Marias River valley has produced period .50-70 Springfield Government cartridge casings and cavalry equipment from the 1870 operation.

name: Battle of Lost River
description: On November 29, 1872, the Modoc War began when U.S. soldiers attempted to arrest Captain Jack's Modoc band at Lost River in Oregon Territory and the confrontation erupted in gunfire, killing soldiers and Modoc warriors and triggering the conflict. The Modoc retreated to the lava beds south of Tule Lake in California. The Lost River battle site near Merrill, Klamath County, Oregon, is on private agricultural land. The Klamath Basin area has produced Indian Wars-era .50 caliber Springfield rounds and military hardware from the 1872-73 Modoc War on private land.

name: Battle of the Lava Beds
description: From November 1872 to June 1873, approximately 60 Modoc warriors under Captain Jack (Kintpuash) defended a labyrinthine volcanic lava formation in northern California against repeated Army assaults by forces five to ten times larger — twice routing the soldiers in disastrous frontal attacks through the treacherous terrain. The Modoc War cost the lives of two generals (including Canby, the only general killed in the Indian Wars) and ended only when Captain Jack was betrayed by members of his own band. Lava Beds National Monument in Siskiyou and Modoc counties, California, preserves the remarkable lava fortress with hiking access to Captain Jack's Stronghold and the cave system where the Modoc held off the Army. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national monument, but the surrounding private land in the lava bed region has produced period .50-70 cartridge casings and military hardware.

name: Battle of Dry Lake
description: On May 10, 1873, U.S. soldiers caught up with the surviving Modoc warriors at Dry Lake in northern California after Captain Jack's band had fragmented and members began surrendering or fighting separately. The engagement at Dry Lake in Modoc County effectively ended the Modoc War's active combat phase, with the final Modoc leaders captured within weeks and Captain Jack eventually hanged. The Dry Lake area in Modoc County, California, is remote high desert country near Tule Lake. The surrounding region of the Modoc War campaign has produced Indian Wars cartridge casings and military equipment on private and public land from the 1872-73 conflict.

name: Battle of the Big Dry Wash
description: On July 17, 1882, the last significant engagement of the Apache Wars in Arizona occurred at Big Dry Wash (General Springs Canyon) on the Mogollon Rim, when U.S. cavalry under Captains Chaffee and MacGowan ambushed a band of White Mountain Apache warriors who had broken out of the San Carlos Reservation. The warriors were routed with heavy casualties, ending White Mountain Apache armed resistance. The Big Dry Wash battlefield is in the Coconino National Forest on the Mogollon Rim near General Springs in Yavapai County, Arizona. Metal detecting is not permitted in national forests without special permit, but the remote Rim Country surrounding area has produced Indian Wars .45-70 Springfield and .45-55 cartridge casings from the 1882 campaign.

name: Battle of Turret Peak
description: On March 27, 1873, Captain George Randall's scouts located and attacked a Yavapai Apache band on the summit of Turret Peak in the Sierra Ancha of central Arizona Territory, killing approximately 23 warriors in a surprise attack that was part of General Crook's Tonto Basin Campaign — the systematic destruction of Apache bands throughout the central Arizona mountains. The Tonto Basin Campaign brought hundreds of Apache in for surrender in the winter and spring of 1873. Turret Peak in Maricopa County, Arizona, is a remote desert mountain accessible via forest service roads. The surrounding Tonto National Forest and private ranch lands have produced Indian Wars .45-55 and .50 caliber cartridge casings from Crook's 1872-73 Apache campaign.

name: Battle of Skeleton Canyon
description: In September 1886, Geronimo and his small band of Apache warriors surrendered to General Nelson Miles at Skeleton Canyon in the Peloncillo Mountains along the Arizona-New Mexico border — ending the last significant armed resistance to U.S. authority in the continental United States and closing the Apache Wars. The surrender of Geronimo (who had eluded 5,000 soldiers and 500 Apache scouts for months) was one of the most publicized military events of the late 19th century. Skeleton Canyon in Hidalgo County, New Mexico, is accessible via private ranches and BLM land in the remote Peloncillo Mountains. The surrounding Peloncillo and San Bernardino Valley area has produced Indian Wars .45-70 cartridge casings and cavalry equipment from the final Apache campaigns on private and public land.

name: Battle of Apache Pass
description: In February 1861, the Bascom Affair at Apache Pass (Stein's Peak area) in the Dos Cabezas Mountains of Arizona Territory ignited a decade of Apache warfare when Lieutenant George Bascom accused Cochise of kidnapping a settler's child and attempted to take him hostage — Cochise escaped, and the retaliatory killings of hostages on both sides launched the bloodiest chapter of the Apache Wars. The Apache Pass itself on the route of the Butterfield Overland Mail in Cochise County, Arizona, preserves Fort Bowie National Historic Site, accessible via a 1.5-mile trail through the rocky desert. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national historic site, but surrounding Cochise County private ranch land has produced Indian Wars-era .50 caliber Springfield rounds, Sharps carbine casings, and cavalry equipment.

name: Battle of Manila Bay
description: On May 1, 1898, Commodore George Dewey's Asiatic Squadron steamed into Manila Bay and destroyed the Spanish fleet in a one-sided naval battle — the opening engagement of the Spanish-American War in the Pacific — fighting for seven hours before the last Spanish ship was silenced, sinking ten warships with no American deaths from enemy fire. The victory gave the United States control of the Philippines and launched a Pacific empire. Manila Bay in the Philippines is a major commercial harbor today, with the historic battle site largely in the shipping lanes off Cavite Naval Station. The original Spanish ships (now extensively salvaged) lie in the bay bottom as historic underwater sites, and period naval hardware has been recovered by divers and fishermen over the decades.

name: Battle of San Juan Hill
description: On July 1, 1898, the crowning engagement of the Cuba campaign saw American regulars and Theodore Roosevelt's Rough Riders (officially the 1st Volunteer Cavalry) assault the Spanish-held heights above Santiago de Cuba — the charge up Kettle Hill (adjacent to San Juan Hill proper) made Roosevelt a national hero and propelled him toward the presidency. The coordinated assault including Black regular Army units of the 9th and 10th Cavalry captured the heights at heavy cost but opened the road to Santiago. San Juan Hill is now El Viso Hill in Santiago, Cuba, with the famous Charge monument and American Cemetery on the site. Access to Cuban battlefields is limited, but the surrounding Santiago area has produced period .30-40 Krag Springfield rifle casings and Spanish Mauser rounds from the 1898 campaign.

name: Battle of Kettle Hill
description: On July 1, 1898, Theodore Roosevelt led the charge up Kettle Hill — the adjacent position to San Juan Hill that is actually shown in most famous photographs of 'the charge' — in what he later called 'the greatest day of my life.' The Rough Riders and Black cavalry of the 9th and 10th regiments broke the Spanish position and joined the main assault on San Juan Hill. Kettle Hill and San Juan Hill are preserved as El Viso Hill park in Santiago, Cuba, with monuments and the American military cemetery. The surrounding Santiago Province has produced Spanish-American War-era .30-40 Krag and 7mm Mauser cartridge casings on private land from the July 1898 campaign.

name: Battle of Las Guasimas
description: On June 24, 1898, the first land engagement of the Cuba campaign was fought at Las Guasimas when dismounted Rough Riders and Regular Army cavalry pushed through the Cuban jungle toward Santiago, clashing with Spanish rear guard troops. The battle was tactically inconclusive but gave the Americans experience in Cuban terrain before the decisive battles at San Juan Heights. Las Guasimas in Santiago Province, Cuba, is accessible by rural road near the village of Siboney. The coastal Santiago Province has produced Spanish-American War-era cartridge casings from both American and Spanish forces on private land near the 1898 campaign routes.

name: Battle of El Caney
description: On July 1, 1898 — the same day as San Juan Hill — General William Shafter ordered a secondary assault on the small Spanish blockhouse fort at El Caney, which was expected to fall in two hours but held out all day against 6,600 American soldiers, delaying the entire campaign. The 500-man Spanish garrison under General Vara del Rey fought stubbornly until overwhelmed. El Caney, now a suburb northeast of Santiago de Cuba, preserves a small museum and monument at the fortified blockhouse site. The Santiago Province countryside has produced Spanish-American War cartridge casings and military hardware from the July 1898 operations.

name: Battle of Santiago de Cuba (Naval)
description: On July 3, 1898, Spanish Admiral Cervera attempted to break out of the blockaded Santiago de Cuba harbor with his four cruisers and two destroyers — all were sunk or beached by the waiting American fleet under Admiral William Sampson and Commodore Winfield Schley in a three-hour battle along the Cuban coast. The destruction of the Spanish Caribbean fleet ended Spain's ability to continue the war in Cuba. The battle was fought in the waters off the southern Cuba coast west of Santiago, with the wrecks of Cervera's ships scattered along the rocky shoreline. The Spanish shipwrecks have been extensively dived and salvaged over the decades, with period naval artifacts recovered by Cuban and international divers.

name: Battle of Guantanamo Bay
description: In June 1898, U.S. Marines landed at Guantanamo Bay on the southeastern coast of Cuba in the war's first amphibious landing, fighting off Spanish forces and establishing the base that would be used to support the Santiago campaign. The engagement included a famous incident where Stephen Crane (author of 'The Red Badge of Courage') was under fire reporting the battle for the New York World. Guantanamo Bay Naval Station has occupied the site continuously since 1898, now known primarily for its post-2001 detention facility. The base and surrounding area are U.S. military property; the Cuban side of the bay boundary is accessible to Cuban nationals.

name: Battle of Manila
description: On August 13, 1898, American forces under General Wesley Merritt attacked and captured the walled city of Manila from Spanish defenders in a staged battle — a secret arrangement between the Spanish and American commanders to allow a formal surrender to Americans rather than to Emilio Aguinaldo's Filipino forces, setting the stage for the subsequent Philippine-American War. The Battle of Manila ended the Spanish-American War in the Philippines and began American colonial rule. The Intramuros (walled city of Manila) in modern Manila, Philippines, preserves significant Spanish colonial fortifications that survived World War II damage, with the Fort Santiago complex open to visitors. The Manila Bay area and surrounding provincial land have produced Spanish-American War-era and Philippine-American War artifacts during construction and archaeological work.

name: Battle of Gonzales
description: On October 2, 1835, the first shots of the Texas Revolution were fired at Gonzales, Texas, when Mexican cavalry attempted to recover a cannon loaned to the Gonzales colonists for defense against Indians. The defiant Texans flew a flag reading 'Come and Take It' and fired the cannon at the Mexican soldiers, who withdrew — a defiance that triggered the broader revolt. Gonzales is a small Texas city on the Guadalupe River, with the Come and Take It cannon and a small downtown historical district commemorating the 'Lexington of Texas.' The surrounding Gonzales County private farmland has produced period Mexican musket balls and early Texas military hardware from the 1835 operations.

name: Battle of the Alamo
description: From February 23 to March 6, 1836, approximately 189 Texas defenders including Davy Crockett, Jim Bowie, and William Barret Travis held the fortified Alamo mission in San Antonio against General Santa Anna's army of 2,000-6,000 Mexican soldiers for 13 days before being overwhelmed and killed in a final assault — their deaths rallied Texas and the world to the cause of Texas independence, 'Remember the Alamo' becoming the battle cry. The Alamo (formally Mission San Antonio de Valero) is today the most visited historic site in Texas, preserved in downtown San Antonio by the Daughters of the Republic of Texas (now under State of Texas management). Metal detecting is not permitted at the Alamo and surrounding state park grounds, but the broader Bexar County area has produced Mexican and Texan musket balls, uniform hardware, and military artifacts on private land from the 1836 campaign.

name: Battle of Goliad (Coleto Creek)
description: On March 19-20, 1836, Colonel James Fannin's force of 300 Texan soldiers was surrounded at Coleto Creek in Victoria County, Texas, by General Urrea's Mexican army while retreating from Goliad, and surrendered after a pitched battle in the open prairie. On Palm Sunday, March 27, Santa Anna's order to execute prisoners was carried out and approximately 342 Texan prisoners were marched out and shot — the Goliad Massacre. The Presidio La Bahía and Mission Espíritu Santo near Goliad, Texas, are remarkably preserved Colonial and Revolution-era structures, with the massacre site marked on the grounds. Metal detecting is not permitted at the historic sites, but surrounding Goliad County private farmland has produced period Mexican and Texan musket balls and military hardware.

name: Battle of San Jacinto
description: On April 21, 1836, General Sam Houston's Texas army caught Santa Anna's force napping during their afternoon siesta at San Jacinto, TX, and launched an 18-minute charge that routed and destroyed the Mexican army — killing or capturing over 1,500 men while suffering only 9 killed, and capturing Santa Anna himself the following day. 'Remember the Alamo, Remember Goliad!' was the battle cry of the charge that secured Texas independence. San Jacinto Battleground State Historic Site near La Marque, TX, preserves the battlefield with the 570-foot San Jacinto Monument (taller than the Washington Monument), the battleship Texas, and the original April 21 terrain. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but surrounding Harris and Galveston County private land has produced period Mexican and Texan musket balls and military hardware from the decisive independence battle.

name: Battle of Refugio
description: In March 1836, a small force of Texan volunteers under Captain Amon King was sent to rescue settlers near Refugio Mission, only to be caught between Mexican forces from multiple directions. King's force was destroyed, and most survivors were executed — part of the series of Texas Revolution disasters in March 1836 preceding the Alamo and Goliad. Mission Nuestra Señora del Refugio in Refugio, Texas, is preserved as a historic site in Refugio County. The surrounding South Texas ranch country has produced period Mexican musket balls and military hardware from the 1836 Texas Revolution operations.

name: Battle of Concepción
description: On October 28, 1835, in the first significant Texan offensive action of the revolution, approximately 90 Texas volunteers under Jim Bowie and James Fannin defeated a Mexican force of 275-400 soldiers near Mission Concepción just south of San Antonio, using the river bank's edge for cover in one of the early demonstrations of Texan riflemen's effectiveness against Mexican formations. Mission Concepción, the oldest unrestored stone church in America, still stands in San Antonio today as part of San Antonio Missions National Historical Park. Metal detecting is not permitted in the national park, but surrounding Bexar County private land has produced period musket balls and military hardware from the 1835 Bexar campaign.

name: Siege of Bexar
description: In November-December 1835, the Army of Texas besieged and captured San Antonio de Béxar, the main Mexican military base in Texas, in a siege culminating in three days of house-to-house fighting through the city's flat-roofed buildings before General Cos surrendered on December 9. The victory cleared central Texas of Mexican military power, though Santa Anna's subsequent invasion would reverse the situation in early 1836. San Antonio is a modern metropolitan area incorporating multiple historical sites including the Alamo, the four surviving missions, and the de la Villita historic district, all in Bexar County. The broader San Antonio area has produced period Mexican and Texan musket balls and military hardware during construction projects.

name: Battle of Stillman's Run
description: On May 14, 1832, Illinois militia under Major Isaiah Stillman fled in panic from a small band of Sac warriors under Black Hawk who had approached under a white flag to negotiate — the militia's rout (and the killing of three Sac warriors sent as messengers under the flag) launched the Black Hawk War and gave the battle its derisive name. Abraham Lincoln, then a militia captain, arrived at the battlefield the next day to help bury the dead. The Stillman Valley battlefield in Ogle County, Illinois, has a small cemetery and historical marker at the approximate site. The surrounding Rock River valley agricultural land has produced early 19th-century musket balls and military hardware from the 1832 Black Hawk War operations.

name: Battle of Wisconsin Heights
description: On July 21, 1832, Wisconsin militia under Colonel Henry Dodge attacked Black Hawk's band on the bluffs above the Wisconsin River near present-day Sauk City, WI — the warriors fought a fierce rear-guard action to protect women, children, and elderly crossing the river on makeshift rafts during the night. The battle was part of Black Hawk's desperate retreat toward the Mississippi, which would end in the massacre at Bad Axe two weeks later. Wisconsin Heights Battlefield State Historical Site in Sauk County, Wisconsin, preserves a portion of the bluff terrain with markers and trails. The surrounding Sauk County agricultural land along the Wisconsin River has produced early 19th-century musket balls and military hardware from the 1832 campaign.

name: Battle of Bad Axe (Black Hawk War)
description: On August 2, 1832, the Black Hawk War ended in a massacre at the mouth of the Bad Axe River (present-day Vernon County, WI) where Black Hawk's band — reduced from over 1,000 to perhaps 500 exhausted, starving survivors — attempted to cross the Mississippi River and was cut down by soldiers and an armed steamboat, killing approximately 150 warriors, women, and children while many others drowned or were captured. Black Hawk himself surrendered shortly after. The Bad Axe battlefield near Victory, Wisconsin, is marked by a state historical monument, and the Mississippi River bluffs terrain of the site is a striking landscape. The surrounding Vernon County agricultural land along the Mississippi has produced early 19th-century musket balls and military hardware from the final battle of the Black Hawk War.

name: Battle of Pecatonica
description: On June 16, 1832, a company of Wisconsin volunteers under Colonel Henry Dodge caught up with a Kickapoo raiding party in the rolling hills near the Pecatonica River in present-day Green County, Wisconsin, killing all eleven warriors in a sharp skirmish — one of the few decisive early engagements of the Black Hawk War. The battle helped establish Dodge's reputation and contributed to his political career in Wisconsin Territory. The Pecatonica battle site near Wiota in Lafayette County, Wisconsin, is private agricultural land in the Driftless Area. The surrounding Green and Lafayette county farmland has produced early 19th-century musket balls from the Black Hawk War period.

name: Battle of Okeechobee
description: On December 25, 1837, the largest battle of the Second Seminole War was fought at Lake Okeechobee, Florida, when Colonel Zachary Taylor led 800 soldiers through a mile of sawgrass swamp to attack Billy Bowlegs's Seminole warriors entrenched in a dense hammock on the lake shore, suffering 26 killed and 112 wounded. Taylor's technical 'victory' sent him on the path toward the presidency ('Old Rough and Ready'), though the Seminoles escaped into the Everglades. Okeechobee Battlefield Historic State Park near Okeechobee, FL, preserves a portion of the battlefield terrain. The Lake Okeechobee area in Okeechobee County, FL, has produced period musket balls and military hardware from the extensive Seminole War operations in south-central Florida.

name: Battle of Lake Apopka
description: In the spring of 1837, U.S. forces operating in central Florida encountered Seminole resistance near Lake Apopka in Orange County as part of the ongoing Second Seminole War operations through the Florida interior. The Lake Apopka area saw military operations as the Army attempted to clear the central Florida region of Seminole resistance during the prolonged guerrilla war. Lake Apopka in Orange County, FL, is now a significant restoration project (formerly one of Florida's most polluted lakes); the surrounding private land and state wildlife management area have produced period musket balls and Seminole War-era military hardware. The Second Seminole War (1835-1842) left artifacts across a vast swath of central and south Florida.

name: Dade Massacre
description: On December 28, 1835, a column of 108 U.S. soldiers under Major Francis Dade was ambushed and nearly annihilated by approximately 180 Seminole warriors under Micanopy, Alligator, and Jumper near present-day Bushnell, FL, as the soldiers marched from Fort Brooke (Tampa) to Fort King (Ocala) — only three men survived the initial assault. The Dade Massacre launched the Second Seminole War, the longest and most costly Indian war in American history. Dade Battlefield Historic State Park near Bushnell, FL, preserves a remarkable 80-acre site with reconstructed breastworks and the graves of the soldiers who fell. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Sumter County private agricultural land has produced period musket balls and military hardware from the 1835 operations.

name: Battle of Wahoo Swamp
description: On November 21, 1836, General Richard Call led a force of Florida militia and U.S. regulars into Wahoo Swamp along the Withlacoochee River in Sumter County, FL, driving Osceola's Seminole warriors from their hammock stronghold — a rare offensive success in the notoriously frustrating Seminole War. The Withlacoochee River swamps provided ideal defensive terrain for the Seminole, and the Army rarely dislodged them. The Wahoo Swamp battle site in Sumter County, FL, is within the Withlacoochee State Forest, accessible by forest trails but with no formal battlefield preservation. The surrounding Withlacoochee watershed private land has produced Second Seminole War musket balls and military hardware.

name: Battle of Hatcheelustee Creek
description: In January 1837, U.S. forces operating in south-central Florida clashed with Seminole warriors at Hatcheelustee Creek in Columbia County as part of the ongoing effort to suppress the Second Seminole War and drive the Seminole south of the Withlacoochee River. The engagement was one of dozens of small fights spread across north Florida's swampy terrain during the grinding guerrilla conflict. The Hatcheelustee Creek area in Columbia County, FL, is largely private and state forest land near Lake City. The north Florida private farmland and former military reservation land has produced Second Seminole War period musket balls and military hardware from the extensive 1835-42 campaign.

name: Battle of Loxahatchee
description: On January 15, 1838, General Thomas Jesup led approximately 1,500 soldiers in an assault across the Loxahatchee River in south Florida, driving Seminole warriors from a prepared position in the dense saw palmetto scrub — one of the largest engagements of the Second Seminole War in south Florida. The battle area is now within the Jonathan Dickinson State Park area in Martin County, FL. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but the surrounding Martin and Palm Beach County private land has produced period musket balls and military hardware from Jesup's 1837-38 south Florida campaign.

name: Battle of Columbus (New Mexico)
description: In the predawn hours of March 9, 1916, approximately 485 Mexican revolutionary troops under Pancho Villa crossed the border and attacked the town of Columbus, NM, and the adjacent Camp Furlong — the last time a foreign military force attacked the continental United States — killing 18 Americans before being driven off by the 13th Cavalry. The raid triggered General Pershing's Punitive Expedition into Mexico to capture Villa, which failed after nearly a year of chasing. Columbus Historic State Monument in Columbus, Luna County, NM, preserves the town's 1916-era buildings with an excellent museum interpreting the raid and Punitive Expedition. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state monument, but surrounding Luna County private desert land has produced .30-06 Springfield and 7mm Mauser cartridge casings from the 1916 engagement.

name: Battle of Carrizal
description: On June 21, 1916, during Pershing's Punitive Expedition, a troop of the 10th Cavalry (Buffalo Soldiers) attempted to pass through the town of Carrizal in Chihuahua, Mexico, and was attacked by Mexican Carrancista troops, resulting in a firefight that killed two American officers and nine soldiers. The incident nearly triggered full-scale war with Mexico. The Carrizal battle site is in a small agricultural community in Chihuahua state, Mexico, near the Chihuahua desert. The incident site is accessible via Mexican federal roads, and the surrounding Chihuahua valley has produced period cartridge casings from the Punitive Expedition operations.

name: Battle of Ojos Azules
description: On May 5, 1916, during Pershing's Punitive Expedition into Mexico, a squadron of the 11th Cavalry under Major Robert Howze attacked Villista forces at Ojos Azules ranch in Chihuahua, Mexico, in a successful saber charge — one of the last mounted cavalry charges by the U.S. Army in its history — killing approximately 61 Villistas and dispersing the force. The engagement was a tactical success in the otherwise frustrating expedition. The Ojos Azules hacienda is in the remote Chihuahua sierra, accessible via rough mountain roads. The Chihuahua mountain country has produced Punitive Expedition-era .30-06 Springfield and .30-40 Krag cartridge casings from the 1916 operations.

name: Battle of Athens (Alabama)
description: On September 23-24, 1864, Confederate General Nathan Bedford Forrest's cavalry raid into northern Alabama captured the town of Athens and its small Union garrison, briefly threatening the vital Nashville & Decatur Railroad that was Sherman's supply line. Forrest bluffed the Union commander into surrendering with a greatly inflated account of his forces. The town of Athens, AL, in Limestone County is a well-preserved antebellum community; the courthouse square and surrounding streets saw the Confederate cavalry operations. Surrounding Limestone County private farmland along the railroad corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from Forrest's 1864 Tennessee-Alabama raids.

name: Battle of Decatur (Alabama)
description: On October 26-29, 1864, Confederate General Forrest's force was repulsed attempting to cross the Tennessee River at Decatur, AL — a Union garrison of about 5,000 held off Forrest's cavalry for three days before the Confederates gave up and moved toward Spring Hill and Franklin. The battle demonstrated that Forrest's cavalry was not invincible against prepared defenses. Decatur, AL, in Morgan County is a modern city on the Tennessee River. The surrounding Morgan County private farmland along the river has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the fall 1864 Hood-Forrest operations in northern Alabama.

name: Battle of Sulphur Creek Trestle
description: On September 25, 1864, Confederate cavalry under General Philip Roddey attacked the Sulphur Creek Trestle on the Nashville & Decatur Railroad near Elk River, AL (present-day Hazel Green area in Limestone County), as part of Forrest's raid to destroy Sherman's supply line. The trestle was briefly captured before Union forces retook and repaired it. The Sulphur Creek Trestle site in Limestone County, Alabama, is on private farmland and in the Elk River bottomland. Surrounding Limestone County private farmland has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls from Forrest's September 1864 northern Alabama operations.

name: Battle of Streight's Raid (Day's Gap)
description: On April 30, 1863, Colonel Abel Streight's Union mounted infantry began their ill-fated raid into Alabama by fighting off Confederate cavalry at Day's Gap in Winston County, AL — the opening engagement of the two-week pursuit that ended in Streight's surrender to Forrest at Cedar Bluff. Forrest's dogged pursuit of Streight's mule-mounted force across 200 miles of Alabama mountains became one of the legendary cavalry pursuits of the Civil War. Day's Gap in Winston County, Alabama, is in the remote Hill Country of north-central Alabama near Double Springs. The surrounding Winston County private land and Bankhead National Forest area has produced Civil War cavalry relics from the April 1863 raid.

name: Battle of Corydon
description: On July 9, 1863, John Hunt Morgan's Confederate cavalry swept through Corydon, Indiana — the state's first capital — defeating the local Home Guard in a brief skirmish and briefly occupying the town, making Corydon the site of the only Civil War battle fought in Indiana. Morgan's men looted the town before continuing north toward Ohio. The Battle of Corydon site in Harrison County, Indiana, is preserved by the Corydon Capitol State Historic Site with a monument and interpretive markers in the historic county seat. The surrounding Harrison County private farmland has produced Civil War cavalry relics from Morgan's July 1863 Indiana-Ohio Raid.

name: Battle of Buffington Island
description: On July 19, 1863, Union forces finally caught up with John Hunt Morgan's Confederate cavalry at Buffington Island on the Ohio River in Meigs County, Ohio, cutting off most of Morgan's force as they attempted to cross into West Virginia — Morgan himself escaped with about 360 men but was captured near Salineville a week later. The Battle of Buffington Island ended the deepest Confederate penetration into Northern territory. Buffington Island State Memorial near Portland, Meigs County, Ohio, preserves a small park with interpretive markers on the Ohio River. Surrounding Meigs County private farmland along the river has produced Civil War cavalry relics from the July 1863 pursuit.

name: Battle of Hartsville
description: On December 7, 1862, Confederate General John Hunt Morgan launched a surprise attack on the Union garrison at Hartsville, TN, capturing over 1,800 Federal troops under Colonel Absalom Moore in an early-morning assault that netted horses, weapons, and supplies for the Confederate army in Middle Tennessee. The victory earned Morgan his commission as brigadier general. The Hartsville battlefield in Trousdale County, Tennessee, is largely private agricultural land along the Cumberland River. Surrounding Trousdale County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, buttons, and military hardware from Morgan's 1862 Tennessee operations.

name: Battle of Munfordville
description: On September 14-17, 1862, General Braxton Bragg's Confederate army besieged and captured the Union garrison at Munfordville, KY — on the Green River bridge of the Louisville & Nashville Railroad — in a siege that briefly gave Bragg control of the railroad but also allowed Buell's Union army to slip around him toward Louisville, foiling Bragg's Kentucky invasion strategy. The battle site at Munfordville in Hart County, Kentucky, is preserved by the Green River Civil War Heritage Corridor, with the river bridge site, earthworks, and a small museum. Surrounding Hart County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the September 1862 Kentucky Campaign.

name: Battle of Richmond (Kentucky)
description: On August 29-30, 1862, the Confederate Kentucky invasion began with one of the most complete victories of the war when General Edmund Kirby Smith's force defeated two green Union divisions under General William Nelson at Richmond, KY, killing or wounding 1,100 and capturing nearly 4,000 — approximately 80% of the Federal force. The victory opened Kentucky to Confederate occupation and seemed to portend the loss of the border state. Richmond Battlefield Park in Madison County, Kentucky, preserves a portion of the battlefield with interpretive trails and the Battlefield Visitor Center. Surrounding Madison County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the August 1862 Kentucky Campaign.

name: Battle of Ivy Mountain
description: On November 8-9, 1861, the Battle of Ivy Mountain (or Ivy Creek) near Prestonsburg, KY, saw Union Colonel William 'Bull' Nelson defeat a Confederate force under Colonel John Williams, driving them from eastern Kentucky in one of the early Union successes that helped secure the mountain counties. The engagement was part of the Union effort to clear the Big Sandy River valley. The Ivy Mountain battle site in Floyd County, Kentucky, is in the remote eastern Kentucky mountains along U.S. Highway 23. Surrounding Floyd County private land in the Big Sandy valley has produced Civil War Minié balls from the early 1861-62 eastern Kentucky operations.

name: Battle of Camp Wildcat
description: On October 21, 1861, Confederate General Felix Zollicoffer's force attacked a Union position at Camp Wildcat (Wildcat Mountain) in Laurel County, Kentucky, attempting to break through the Union defensive line in the Cumberland Gap corridor. The attack was repulsed by Union Indiana and Kentucky infantry in a Union defensive victory that helped secure central Kentucky. The Camp Wildcat battlefield in Laurel County, Kentucky, is preserved at Camp Wildcat Civil War Battlefield, a county historic site with trails through the mountain terrain. The surrounding Laurel County mountains in the Cumberland foothills have produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the 1861 eastern Kentucky operations.

name: Battle of Middle Creek
description: On January 10, 1862, General James Garfield (future president of the United States) defeated Confederate General Humphrey Marshall at the Battle of Middle Creek near Prestonsburg, KY, driving the Confederates out of the Big Sandy Valley and securing eastern Kentucky for the Union. The victory launched Garfield's military and political career. Middle Creek National Battlefield in Floyd County, Kentucky, is managed by the National Park Service as a small battlefield with interpretive trails through the wooded ridge terrain. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national battlefield, but surrounding Floyd County private land in the Big Sandy watershed has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the January 1862 operations.

name: Battle of Ivy Creek
description: The Battle of Ivy Creek (sometimes called Ivy Mountain) near Prestonsburg, Kentucky in November 1861 was fought in the rugged Big Sandy River valley as Union forces under Colonel Nelson pushed Confederate General Williams's force south and east toward Virginia. The engagement was among the first in a series of Union operations to secure the mountainous eastern Kentucky region for the Union. The Ivy Creek battle site in Floyd County, Kentucky, is the same general area as Ivy Mountain, in the deeply rural eastern Kentucky mountains. The Big Sandy valley and surrounding Floyd and Pike county private lands have produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the 1861-62 eastern Kentucky campaign.

name: Battle of Lewisburg (West Virginia)
description: On May 23, 1862, a Union force under General George Crook defeated Confederate forces under Colonel George Patton (grandfather of the WWII general) at Lewisburg, WV, in a sharp engagement that temporarily secured the Greenbrier River valley and the vital James River and Kanawha Turnpike. The battle was a significant Union tactical success in the complex western Virginia mountain theater. Lewisburg, WV, is one of the best-preserved antebellum towns in the eastern United States; Confederate Cemetery on McElhenney Road contains the graves of those who fell. The surrounding Greenbrier County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the multiple 1861-64 engagements in the Lewisburg area.

name: Battle of Scary Creek
description: On July 17, 1861, Confederate forces under Colonel George Patton (grandfather of WWII's General Patton) repulsed a Union attack at Scary Creek in Putnam County, West Virginia, in an early engagement of the western Virginia campaign that contested control of the Kanawha Valley. The Confederate victory temporarily halted the Union advance up the Kanawha River. The Scary Creek battle site near Teays, Putnam County, WV, is largely private suburban and rural land along the Kanawha River valley. The Kanawha Valley saw extensive Civil War operations throughout 1861-65, and private land in the area has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware.

name: Battle of Gauley Bridge
description: In September 1861, Union forces under General William Rosecrans and the Confederate forces under Floyd and Wise contested control of the Gauley Bridge area at the confluence of the Gauley and New rivers in Fayette County, WV — a critical position controlling movement into the Virginia mountains and the Kanawha Valley. The Union eventually secured the position and used it as a key base for operations in western Virginia. The Gauley Bridge historic community in Fayette County, WV, preserves several 19th-century buildings; the bridge and river confluence area retain their scenic character. The surrounding New and Gauley river valleys have produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the 1861 mountain campaign.

name: Battle of Cross Lanes
description: On August 26, 1861, Confederate General John Floyd surprised and routed a Union regiment under Colonel Erastus Tyler at Cross Lanes on the Gauley River in Nicholas County, WV, in one of the Confederacy's few early successes in western Virginia. Floyd's victory temporarily threatened Union control of the Kanawha Valley. The Cross Lanes battle site on Carnifex Ferry Road in Nicholas County, WV, is near the more famous Carnifex Ferry battlefield. The surrounding Nicholas County private farmland along the Gauley River has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the 1861 western Virginia operations.

name: Battle of Philippi
description: On June 3, 1861, the Battle of Philippi in Barbour County, WV, was the first land engagement of the Civil War east of the Ohio River — a Union surprise attack on a Confederate camp that sent the Confederates fleeing in what Northern newspapers gleefully called the 'Philippi Races.' A Confederate soldier named James Hanger lost his leg in the battle and subsequently invented the first articulated prosthetic leg. Philippi, WV, preserves the 1852 covered bridge that stood during the battle (still in use), and the Civil War Skirmish Site is marked downtown. Metal detecting is not permitted at the historic site, but surrounding Barbour County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the early 1861 western Virginia campaign.

name: Battle of Corrick's Ford
description: On July 13, 1861, Union forces caught the retreating Confederate General Robert Garnett's force at Corrick's Ford on the Cheat River in Tucker County, WV, killing Garnett — the first general officer killed in the Civil War — as the Confederates fled from the Battle of Rich Mountain. The brief action ended the first phase of the western Virginia campaign. The Corrick's Ford battle site on the Cheat River in Tucker County, WV, is in the remote Cheat River gorge, now accessible via Parsons and the Monongahela National Forest. The surrounding Tucker County mountains have produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from McClellan's 1861 western Virginia campaign.

name: Battle of Hanging Rock Pass
description: On May 10, 1864, Confederate forces of the Army of New River under General Albert Jenkins ambushed a Union cavalry column at Hanging Rock Pass in Monroe County, WV (near Salt Sulphur Springs), inflicting casualties and delaying the Union advance before Crook's main force came up and drove them back. The engagement was part of the prelude to the Battle of Cloyd's Mountain the day before Crook began his attack on Saltville. The Hanging Rock Pass battle site in Monroe County, WV, is in the scenic Greenbrier Valley near the Virginia border. The remote Monroe County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from Crook's 1864 spring raid through the Alleghenies.

name: Battle of Droop Mountain
description: On November 6, 1863, Union General William Averell's cavalry division attacked Confederate forces under General William Jones atop Droop Mountain in Pocahontas County, WV, routing them in the largest Civil War battle fought in what is now West Virginia, and opening the Greenbrier Valley to Union control. Averell's victory also enabled the subsequent destruction of the Virginia & Tennessee Railroad, a critical Confederate supply artery. Droop Mountain Battlefield State Park near Hillsboro, Pocahontas County, WV, preserves the mountain summit terrain with earthwork remains, interpretive trails, and a museum. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state park, but surrounding Pocahontas County private mountain farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the November 1863 operations.

name: Battle of White Sulphur Springs
description: On August 26-27, 1863, Union General William Averell's cavalry raid toward Lewisburg, WV, was stopped at White Sulphur Springs (the famous resort, now The Greenbrier) by Confederate troops under Colonel George Patton in a two-day battle fought around the historic spa grounds. Averell was forced to retreat, limiting the tactical value of his otherwise successful Confederacy-deep penetration. The Greenbrier Resort in White Sulphur Springs, WV, now occupies the original resort property, which saw the Civil War battle in its wooded grounds. The surrounding Greenbrier County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the August 1863 operations in the Greenbrier Valley.

name: Battle of Buffington Ford
description: On July 18-19, 1863, a Union force caught part of Morgan's Confederate cavalry at Buffington Island and Ford on the Ohio River in Meigs County, Ohio — the main engagement where most of Morgan's force was captured or dispersed while attempting to cross the Ohio River back into West Virginia. Morgan himself escaped with a small force but was captured near Salineville days later. Buffington Island State Memorial near Portland, Meigs County, Ohio, is a small riverside park marking the battle site on the Ohio River shore. Surrounding Meigs County private farmland along the river has produced Civil War cavalry relics from Morgan's July 1863 Ohio Raid.

name: Battle of Chancellorsville (Second Day)
description: On May 2, 1863 — the pivotal second day of the Battle of Chancellorsville — Stonewall Jackson's corps completed its famous 12-mile flank march and struck the exposed right flank of the Union XI Corps at dusk, routing two divisions in the biggest single flank attack of the Civil War and creating a crisis for Hooker's army. Jackson was mortally wounded in the darkness returning from a night reconnaissance when his own soldiers fired on his party, a loss that Robert E. Lee called 'my right arm.' Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park preserves the Wilderness Run area, the Chancellor House site, and the spot where Jackson was wounded on the Plank Road. Metal detecting is strictly prohibited in the national park, but surrounding Spotsylvania County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from this crucial engagement.

name: Battle of Paducah
description: On March 25, 1864, Nathan Bedford Forrest's Confederate cavalry swept into Paducah, KY, at the confluence of the Tennessee and Ohio rivers — a major Union supply depot — driving Federal troops into Fort Anderson and demanding surrender. The garrison under Colonel Stephen Hicks refused; Forrest's men plundered the town before withdrawing when ironclad gunboats arrived. The Battle of Paducah site is within the modern city of Paducah, KY, in McCracken County, with a historical marker and River Heritage Museum interpreting the attack. The surrounding McCracken County area along the Ohio River has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from Forrest's 1864 Kentucky operations.

name: Battle of Fort Anderson
description: On February 19-20, 1865, the second Battle of Fort Anderson on the Cape Fear River in Brunswick County, NC, saw Union naval gunfire and infantry pressure force the Confederate garrison to evacuate the earthen fort protecting Wilmington from the south — the Fort Anderson position fell without direct assault as Confederate forces retreated northward. Fort Anderson in Brunswick County, NC (now part of Brunswick Town/Fort Anderson State Historic Site), preserves the remarkable earthwork fortifications with excellent walking trails through the mounds and gun positions. Metal detecting is not permitted in the state historic site, but the surrounding Brunswick County coastal plain private land has produced Civil War artillery fragments and military hardware from the February 1865 Wilmington campaign.

name: Battle of Southwest Creek
description: On March 7-8, 1865, Confederate forces under General Braxton Bragg skirmished with the advance of General John Schofield's Union army at Southwest Creek in Lenoir County, NC, delaying the Union advance toward Goldsborough and the final junction of Union forces in the Carolinas. The engagement was part of the brief Confederate resistance during Sherman's march through North Carolina. The Southwest Creek battle site in Lenoir County, NC, is private agricultural land in the coastal plain near Kinston. Surrounding Lenoir County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the March 1865 Carolinas Campaign operations.

name: Battle of Monroe's Cross Roads
description: On March 10, 1865, Confederate cavalry under Generals Wade Hampton and Joseph Wheeler launched a surprise dawn attack on the camp of Kilpatrick's Union cavalry at Monroe's Cross Roads in Cumberland County, NC, catching the cavalrymen in their bedrolls and nearly capturing Kilpatrick himself, who fled in his nightshirt. Kilpatrick's cavalry reorganized and drove off the Confederates, but it remains the most humiliating near-capture of a Union general of the war. Monroe's Cross Roads Battlefield near Fayetteville, NC, is preserved by Fort Bragg (now Fort Liberty) and accessible to the public through the military installation. Metal detecting is not permitted on the military installation, but surrounding Cumberland County private land has produced Civil War cavalry relics from Sherman's March through North Carolina.

name: Battle of White Oak Road
description: On March 31, 1865, a Union assault along the White Oak Road in Dinwiddie County, VA, broke through the Confederate right flank outside Petersburg before Confederate counterattacks partially restored the line — a prelude to the decisive battle at Five Forks the following day. The battle demonstrated the progressive collapse of the Confederate defensive perimeter around Petersburg. The White Oak Road battlefield is partially preserved within Petersburg National Battlefield's western units in Dinwiddie County, VA, with earthwork traces along the original road corridor. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park areas, but surrounding private farmland in Dinwiddie County has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the April 1865 Appomattox Campaign operations.

name: Battle of Lewis Farm
description: On March 29, 1865, Union forces pushing westward toward the Boydton Plank Road engaged Confederate defenders at Lewis Farm (Quaker Road) in Dinwiddie County, VA, opening the final offensive moves of the Appomattox Campaign. The engagement was the first step in the sequence of battles leading to Five Forks and the fall of Petersburg. The Lewis Farm battle site in Dinwiddie County, VA, is largely private farmland with Civil War Trails markers. Surrounding private land in Dinwiddie County has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the intense March-April 1865 Petersburg Campaign.

name: Battle of Dinwiddie Court House
description: On March 31, 1865, Sheridan's cavalry was pushed back through the crossroads at Dinwiddie Court House by Confederate cavalry under Fitzhugh Lee and George Pickett — a rare Confederate cavalry success in the final weeks of the war. The following day at Five Forks, the tables turned completely. The Dinwiddie Court House battlefield in Dinwiddie County, VA, is within and near the small courthouse town of Dinwiddie. Civil War Trails interpretive markers and the historic courthouse building can be found along the original road. Surrounding private farmland in Dinwiddie County has produced Civil War cavalry relics from the final Appomattox Campaign operations.

name: Battle of Namozine Church
description: On April 3, 1865, the first day of the Appomattox Campaign proper, Union cavalry and infantry caught the Confederate rear guard at Namozine Church in Amelia County, VA, capturing hundreds of men in the first significant action of Lee's desperate retreat westward from Petersburg and Richmond. The battle site is near the historic Namozine Church (still standing) in Amelia County, VA. The surrounding Amelia County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and discarded military equipment from Lee's April 1865 retreat route — this corridor has been moderately productive for private land detecting.

name: Battle of Amelia Springs
description: On April 5, 1865, Union cavalry intercepted Confederate cavalry and wagons at Amelia Springs in Amelia County, VA, as Lee's retreating army desperately sought food and the road to Danville was being blocked by Union forces. The engagement added to the pressure of Lee's collapsing march. The Amelia Springs battle site in Amelia County, VA, is largely private farmland and the former spa grounds around Amelia Springs. The Amelia Court House and surrounding Amelia County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and discarded military equipment from Lee's April 1865 retreat.

name: Battle of Farmville
description: On April 7, 1865, the day after Sayler's Creek, Union forces engaged Confederate rear guards at Farmville, VA, and High Bridge, fighting through the small Southside Railroad town as Lee's retreating army attempted to reach supplies waiting at the depot — only to find the rations could not be distributed before Union pressure forced another retreat. That evening, Grant sent Lee his first note suggesting surrender. The town of Farmville in Prince Edward County, VA, retains its antebellum character with several historic buildings; the High Bridge Trail State Park corridor connects the town to the High Bridge battle site. The surrounding Prince Edward County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and discarded equipment from the retreat.

name: Battle of Appomattox Station
description: On April 8, 1865, the evening before the final surrender, Union cavalry under General George Custer raced ahead of Lee's retreating army and captured the Confederate supply trains waiting at Appomattox Station, VA — destroying Lee's last hope of resupplying his army and completing the encirclement of the Army of Northern Virginia. Lee now had no choice but to surrender or attempt a breakout through the Union cavalry. Appomattox Station is now part of Appomattox Court House National Historical Park in Appomattox County, VA, with the station site marked near the village. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private farmland in Appomattox County has produced Civil War Minié balls and military equipment from the final retreat.

name: Battle of Sailor's Creek
description: Sailor's Creek (also Sayler's Creek) on April 6, 1865, was the largest engagement of the Appomattox Campaign, where Union forces cut off and overwhelmed two Confederate corps in a catastrophic series of battles across three separate fight sites — the naval-gun-supported assault on Ewell's corps at Hillsman's Farm, the routing of Anderson's corps at Marshall's Crossroads, and the defeat of Gordon's rear guard at Lockett's Farm. The loss of nearly 8,000 men — a quarter of Lee's remaining army — made Appomattox inevitable. Sayler's Creek Battlefield State Historical Park in Prince Edward and Amelia counties, VA, preserves portions of the three battle sites with interpretive trails. Surrounding private land along the Sailor's Creek corridor has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the final campaign.

name: Battle of Gravelly Run
description: On March 29, 1865, the opening day of the final Appomattox Campaign, Union troops under General Gouverneur Warren skirmished with Confederate defenders at Gravelly Run (Quaker Road) in Dinwiddie County, VA, as the Union army moved to extend its lines west and cut the Southside Railroad. The engagement was the prelude to the larger battles at White Oak Road and Five Forks. The Gravelly Run battle site in Dinwiddie County, VA, is private farmland along the stream. Surrounding Dinwiddie County private land has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the final Petersburg Campaign operations of March-April 1865.

name: Battle of Totopotomoy Creek
description: On May 28-30, 1864, Grant's army clashed with Lee's forces along Totopotomoy Creek in Hanover County, VA, as Grant continued his relentless effort to find a way to flank Lee's army and reach Richmond. The fighting along Totopotomoy Creek was part of the bloody sequence of battles that would culminate at Cold Harbor. The Totopotomoy Creek battlefield in Hanover County, VA, is preserved by the American Battlefield Trust and Hanover County, with hiking trails along the creek corridor. Metal detecting is not permitted on the preserved land, but surrounding private Hanover County farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls, artillery fragments, and military hardware from the May-June 1864 Overland Campaign.

name: Battle of Old Church
description: On May 30, 1864, Union and Confederate cavalry clashed at Old Church in Hanover County, VA, as the armies jockeyed for position following the battle at Totopotomoy Creek — Sheridan's cavalry drove off Confederate horsemen near the historic Old Church meetinghouse. The battle was part of the preliminary maneuvering before Cold Harbor. The Old Church area near Mechanicsville in Hanover County, VA, retains the historic church building and surrounding rural character. Surrounding private farmland in Hanover County has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls from the May 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Hawes' Shop
description: On May 28, 1864, the largest cavalry battle of the Overland Campaign was fought at Hawes' Shop (Enon Church) in Hanover County, VA, when Sheridan's cavalry met Confederate cavalry in an all-day dismounted firefight — the new Union Spencer repeating carbines were used in action for the first time in substantial numbers, demonstrating their overwhelming firepower advantage. The battle site at Enon Church in Hanover County, VA, is private farmland; Enon Church Road follows the original route. The surrounding Hanover County private agricultural land has produced Civil War cavalry relics including Spencer cartridge casings, Minié balls, and military hardware from the May 1864 campaign.

name: Battle of Haw's Shop
description: The Battle of Haw's Shop (sometimes Hawes' Shop) on May 28, 1864, in Hanover County, VA, was a prolonged cavalry engagement where Sheridan's horsemen used new Spencer repeating carbines to fight Confederate cavalry to a standstill in the dense Virginia woods. The battle confirmed the superiority of breech-loading repeaters over single-shot carbines and demonstrated the growing tactical capability of the Union cavalry. The Haw's Shop area near Studley, Hanover County, VA, is private agricultural land. The Hanover County corridor between the North Anna and Cold Harbor has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls on private land from the Overland Campaign.

name: Battle of Trevilian Station
description: On June 11-12, 1864, the largest all-cavalry battle of the Civil War was fought at Trevilian Station (Louisa Court House), VA, when Sheridan's cavalry raided the Virginia Central Railroad and was intercepted by Confederate cavalry under Hampton and Fitzhugh Lee — the two-day battle cost both sides over 1,000 casualties and forced Sheridan to abandon his raid and retreat. Trevilian Station battlefield in Louisa County, VA, is partially preserved by the American Battlefield Trust with walking access to key positions. Metal detecting is not permitted on the preserved land, but surrounding Louisa County private farmland has produced Civil War cavalry relics including carbine casings, saber fragments, and horse equipment from the intense June 1864 cavalry battle.

name: Battle of St. Mary's Church
description: On June 24, 1864, Confederate cavalry under Wade Hampton caught Sheridan's cavalry at St. Mary's Church in Charles City County, VA, during the Union cavalry's return from the Trevilian Station raid, forcing Sheridan to fight off repeated Confederate attacks while crossing the James River. The engagement cost both sides significant casualties. St. Mary's Church in Charles City County, VA, is the historic Colonial-era church that still stands; the surrounding landscape retains its 18th and 19th-century rural character. The Charles City County private farmland in the James River tidewater has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls from the June-July 1864 operations.

name: Battle of Deep Bottom (First)
description: On July 27-29, 1864, the first Battle of Deep Bottom on the James River north of Richmond saw Union General Hancock's Second Corps cross the James and push toward Richmond, attempting to draw Confederate reserves away from the Petersburg lines before the Crater assault. The operation achieved limited gains before withdrawing. Deep Bottom in Henrico County, VA, is now a boat landing and open space area along the James River; the fortification remains are part of Richmond National Battlefield Park. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park, but surrounding private farmland in Henrico County along the James has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware.

name: Battle of Burgess Mill
description: On October 27-28, 1864, Grant's attempt to cut the Boydton Plank Road at Burgess Mill in Dinwiddie County, VA, involved Hancock's corps fighting toward the road when Confederate counterattacks cut the Union force in two — Hancock was able to extricate his command but the road remained in Confederate hands. Burgess Mill (Dabney's Mill area) in Dinwiddie County is within the western Petersburg National Battlefield units, with earthwork remains visible. Metal detecting is prohibited in the national park areas, but surrounding Dinwiddie County private farmland has produced Civil War Minié balls and military hardware from the late-war Petersburg operations.

name: Battle of Stony Creek Depot
description: On June 29, 1864, Confederate cavalry under General William Lee attacked a Union cavalry force destroying the Weldon Railroad at Stony Creek Depot in Sussex County, VA — part of Wilson's Raid to cut Confederate supply lines to Petersburg. The Confederate counterattack, combined with the action at Reams Station the same day, forced Wilson's cavalry to abandon its captured guns and fight through Confederate encirclement to reach Union lines. The Stony Creek Depot battlefield in Sussex County, VA, is along the Weldon Railroad corridor on private farmland. Surrounding Sussex County private land has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls from Wilson's June 1864 Weldon Railroad Raid.

name: Battle of Ream's Station (First)
description: On June 22-23, 1864, the first Battle of Reams Station on the Weldon Railroad in Dinwiddie County, VA, saw Union cavalry under Wilson attempting to destroy the railroad as far south as possible when Confederate cavalry under Hampton attacked and drove off the Union troopers, reclaiming the destroyed track positions. The engagement was the opening phase of the prolonged contest for the Weldon Railroad that continued through August 1864. The first Reams Station battle site in Dinwiddie County, VA, is private farmland near Dinwiddie. Surrounding private land in the Weldon Railroad corridor has produced Civil War cavalry relics and Minié balls from the summer 1864 Petersburg operations.
